That forced operators to leave the dashboard to understand whether ingestion delay or replay pressure was building. | Added dashboard panels for backlog age plus active reprocessing keys and for reprocessing worker claimed/completed/failed throughput by job type. | If operators later need a dedicated ingestion recovery dashboard, split backlog/replay/DLQ/worker signals into a focused recovery view rather than endlessly growing the overview dashboard. | Evidence: [CR-080-INGESTION-BACKLOG-REPROCESSING-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md](./CR-080-INGESTION-BACKLOG-REPROCESSING-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md); `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`. |
| CR-081 | 2026-03-11 | Outbox and valuation dashboard coverage | Hardened | Outbox backlog/publish pressure and valuation scheduler/worker health metrics were already exported, but the bundled Grafana overview did not surface them. Operators therefore had no direct dashboard signal for event publication buildup or valuation worker health. | Added dashboard panels for outbox pending/published/failed/retried pressure and for valuation job creation, terminal failures, worker claim rate, and stale reset rate. | If the overview becomes too crowded, move the operational reliability panels into a dedicated dashboard rather than removing them entirely. | Evidence: [CR-081-OUTBOX-VALUATION-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md](./CR-081-OUTBOX-VALUATION-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md); `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`. |
| CR-082 | 2026-03-11 | Timeseries feature doc ownership drift | Hardened | The feature docs under `docs/features/timeseries_generator/` still described `timeseries_generator_service` as owning both position and portfolio time-series stages even after portfolio aggregation moved to `portfolio_aggregation_service`. This sent readers to the wrong ownership model and to stale file paths. | Updated the overview, methodology guide, and developer guide to describe the split correctly and corrected contributor file/test paths to the live `portfolio_aggregation_service` sources where appropriate. | If the split grows further, consider separate feature-doc directories for position-timeseries generation and portfolio aggregation. | Evidence: [CR-082-TIMESERIES-FEATURE-DOC-OWNERSHIP-DRIFT-REVIEW.md](./CR-082-TIMESERIES-FEATURE-DOC-OWNERSHIP-DRIFT-REVIEW.md); `docs/features/timeseries_generator/01_Feature_Timeseries_Generator_Overview.md`; `docs/features/timeseries_generator/03_Methodology_Guide.md`; `docs/features/timeseries_generator/05_Developer_Guide.md`. |
| CR-083 | 2026-03-11 | Integration schema skip cleanup | Hardened | A small set of integration tests still used `pytest.skip(...)` when active schema objects were absent, even though those tables and columns are now part of the supported baseline. That weakened the integration tier and could hide migration regressions. | Replaced the schema-guard skips with explicit assertions for required tables and columns so the integration tests now fail loudly on schema drift instead of silently bypassing coverage. | If future schema evolution introduces optional surfaces, handle that at the fixture/profile level rather than with ad hoc skips inside integration tests. | Evidence: [CR-083-INTEGRATION-SCHEMA-SKIP-CLEANUP-REVIEW.md](./CR-083-INTEGRATION-SCHEMA-SKIP-CLEANUP-REVIEW.md); `tests/integration/services/calculators/cost_calculator_service/test_int_cost_repository_lot_offset.py`; `tests/integration/services/persistence_service/repositories/test_repositories.py`. |
| CR-084 | 2026-03-11 | Analytics export dashboard coverage | Hardened | Analytics export job outcome, duration, result-size, and page-depth metrics already existed, but the bundled Grafana overview did not surface them. Operators therefore had no dashboard-level visibility into export health or payload growth. | Added dashboard panels for analytics export job outcomes and p95 export depth/latency characteristics by dataset type. | If the overview becomes too crowded, move analytics export panels to a dedicated control-plane dashboard rather than removing visibility. | Evidence: [CR-084-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md](./CR-084-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-DASHBOARD-REVIEW.md); `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`. |
| CR-085 | 2026-03-11 | Database catalog control-plane usage references | Hardened | The schema catalog still cited `query_control_plane_service/app/main.py` as a table-usage reference in several places. That was too coarse to be useful for ownership tracing because `main.py` is only app assembly, not the actual table-owning router/service surface. | Replaced those references with the actual control-plane ownership paths for simulation, integration, and analytics-input contracts. | Keep the catalog pointed at the owning router/service/repository path rather than generic app entrypoints when future refactors land. | Evidence: [CR-085-DATABASE-CATALOG-CONTROL-PLANE-USAGE-REVIEW.md](./CR-085-DATABASE-CATALOG-CONTROL-PLANE-USAGE-REVIEW.md); `docs/Database-Schema-Catalog.md`. |
| CR-086 | 2026-03-11 | Current-state ownership evidence drift | Hardened | A few current-state docs still used `query_control_plane_service/app/main.py` or other service mains as ownership evidence for behavior that is actually implemented in concrete router modules. That weakened traceability in standards and the RFC index. | Replaced the weak evidence references with the live router-level ownership paths in `durability-consistency.md` and the RFC-081 index row. | Keep future implementation evidence pointed at the concrete owning module unless the startup contract itself is the subject. | Evidence: [CR-086-CURRENT-STATE-OWNERSHIP-EVIDENCE-DRIFT-REVIEW.md](./CR-086-CURRENT-STATE-OWNERSHIP-EVIDENCE-DRIFT-REVIEW.md); `docs/standards/durability-consistency.md`; `docs/RFCs/RFC-INDEX.md`. |
| CR-087 | 2026-03-11 | Stale control epoch emission | Hardened | The durable `FINANCIAL_RECONCILIATION` control-stage row was already monotonic, and the read path already chose the latest epoch. The remaining gap was that an older reconciliation completion could still publish a stale `PortfolioDayControlsEvaluated` event after a newer epoch already existed for the same portfolio-day. | Added latest-epoch resolution to `PipelineStageRepository` and suppressed stale control-event emission in `PipelineOrchestratorService` when the incoming completion is not for the latest epoch. Added unit and integration coverage for the stale-vs-latest epoch invariant. | Apply the same review pattern to any future control-plane event that persists multiple epochs for the same business scope: durable monotonicity is not enough if emission semantics are still stale. | Evidence: [CR-087-STALE-CONTROL-EPOCH-EMISSION-REVIEW.md](./CR-087-STALE-CONTROL-EPOCH-EMISSION-REVIEW.md); `src/services/pipeline_orchestrator_service/app/services/pipeline_orchestrator_service.py`; `src/services/pipeline_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/pipeline_stage_repository.py`; `tests/unit/services/pipeline_orchestrator_service/services/test_pipeline_orchestrator_service.py`; `tests/integration/services/pipeline_orchestrator_service/test_int_pipeline_stage_repository.py`. |
| CR-088 | 2026-03-11 | Stale valuation epoch job creation | Hardened | `ValuationJobRepository.upsert_job(...)` previously accepted any epoch for a given `(portfolio_id, security_id, valuation_date)` scope, so an older readiness event could still create or re-arm obsolete valuation work after a newer epoch already existed. | Added latest-epoch resolution to `ValuationJobRepository` and skipped stale older-epoch upserts when a newer epoch already exists for the same valuation scope. Added unit and DB-backed integration coverage for the stale-vs-latest valuation job invariant. | Apply the same stale-epoch review to any other scheduler/work-set repository that keys work by business scope plus epoch. | Evidence: [CR-088-STALE-VALUATION-EPOCH-JOB-REVIEW.md](./CR-088-STALE-VALUATION-EPOCH-JOB-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_valuation_job_repository.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_valuation_repo.py`. |
| CR-089 | 2026-03-11 | Current-epoch valuation trigger selection | Hardened | The immediate price-event path used `find_open_position_keys_for_security_on_date(...)`, which previously returned any open epoch for a key as of the target date. That meant obsolete historical epochs could still be selected as valuation work candidates even when `PositionState` had advanced the key to a newer current epoch. | Tightened `find_open_position_keys_for_security_on_date(...)` to join against `PositionState` and return only the current epoch for each active key. Added DB-backed integration coverage proving mixed historical epochs resolve to the current epoch only. | Apply the same ownership rule to any work-candidate query: if a key has a canonical current-state owner, candidate generation must align with that owner instead of inferring “latest enough” from history alone. | Evidence: [CR-089-CURRENT-EPOCH-VALUATION-TRIGGER-REVIEW.md](./CR-089-CURRENT-EPOCH-VALUATION-TRIGGER-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_valuation_repo.py`. |
| CR-090 | 2026-03-11 | Epoch-fenced watermark advance | Hardened | `bulk_update_states(...)` updated `PositionState` rows by key only, so a scheduler pass that observed epoch `N` could still overwrite the newer current row after the key had already advanced to epoch `N+1`. This was a stale-writer race on canonical current state, not merely stale job creation. | Added `expected_epoch` fencing to `bulk_update_states(...)`, updated the valuation scheduler implementations to pass the observed epoch through with each watermark/status advance, and added unit coverage proving stale expected epochs do not overwrite newer rows and scheduler updates carry the observed epoch. | Apply the same epoch-fence rule to any other bulk state mutation path that derives writes from an earlier scheduler snapshot. | Evidence: [CR-090-EPOCH-FENCED-WATERMARK-ADVANCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-090-EPOCH-FENCED-WATERMARK-ADVANCE-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/position_state_repository.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_position_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-091 | 2026-03-11 | Position valuation worker ownership cleanup | Hardened | `position_valuation_calculator` was already a pure worker runtime, but it still carried dead scheduler, reprocessing-worker, readiness-consumer, price-event-consumer, and worker-local settings files from before the valuation split. These paths were no longer wired into the service runtime and risked future fixes landing in the wrong service. | Deleted the dead orchestration/runtime residue and the orphaned worker-local tests, corrected current-state schema catalog references to the live `valuation_orchestrator_service` ownership surface, fixed stale copied path headers in the surviving orchestrator files, and restored the still-valid consumer/scheduler/reprocessing tests under `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/` so coverage follows the live owner. | Keep `position_valuation_calculator` worker-only. Any future scheduling or replay logic belongs in `valuation_orchestrator_service`, not the worker service. | Evidence: [CR-091-POSITION-VALUATION-WORKER-OWNERSHIP-CLEANUP.md](./CR-091-POSITION-VALUATION-WORKER-OWNERSHIP-CLEANUP.md); `src/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/app/consumer_manager.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/consumer_manager.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/consumers/*`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/*`; `docs/Database-Schema-Catalog.md`. |
| CR-092 | 2026-03-11 | Stale reprocessing job recovery | Hardened | `reprocessing_jobs` tracked attempt metadata but had no stale `PROCESSING` reset path. A dead `ReprocessingWorker` could therefore leave `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs stuck forever, unlike valuation and aggregation jobs which already had stale-processing recovery. | Added `ReprocessingJobRepository.find_and_reset_stale_jobs(timeout_minutes=15)`, wired `ReprocessingWorker` to run that recovery step before claiming the next batch, and added unit coverage for repository stale-reset behavior and worker ordering. | If reprocessing job types expand later, keep them on the same durable stale-reset contract rather than adding type-specific recovery behavior ad hoc. | Evidence: [CR-092-STALE-REPROCESSING-JOB-RECOVERY-REVIEW.md](./CR-092-STALE-REPROCESSING-JOB-RECOVERY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_reprocessing_worker.py`. |
| CR-093 | 2026-03-11 | Position state mutation timestamp integrity | Hardened | `PositionState` is the canonical replay/watermark state table, but its two replay-critical mutation paths did not explicitly refresh `updated_at`. That left timestamp integrity dependent on implicit ORM/update behavior rather than explicit state mutation intent. | Added `updated_at=func.now()` to `increment_epoch_and_reset_watermark(...)` and `update_watermarks_if_older(...)`, and extended DB-backed repository tests to assert the mutated rows keep a populated timestamp. | Keep current-state timestamp fields explicit on all future repository mutation statements instead of relying on implicit column `onupdate` behavior. | Evidence: [CR-093-POSITION-STATE-MUTATION-TIMESTAMP-INTEGRITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-093-POSITION-STATE-MUTATION-TIMESTAMP-INTEGRITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/position_state_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_position_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-094 | 2026-03-11 | Position consumer replay boundary | Hardened | `TransactionEventConsumer` already distinguished stage-gate payloads from replay/original transaction payloads, but there was no explicit unit-level proof that replay payloads bypass canonical transaction lookup while preserving replay epoch and idempotency behavior. | Added consumer-level tests proving the replay path bypasses canonical lookup, preserves the incoming epoch, and still marks idempotency on the direct replay contract path. | Keep future consumer changes aligned to the same boundary: stage-gate events may hydrate canonical rows; replay payloads should not. | Evidence: [CR-094-POSITION-CONSUMER-REPLAY-BOUNDARY-REVIEW.md](./CR-094-POSITION-CONSUMER-REPLAY-BOUNDARY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/consumers/transaction_event_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_calculator/consumers/test_position_calculator_consumer.py`. |
| CR-095 | 2026-03-11 | Replay correlation propagation | Hardened | `TransactionEventConsumer.process_message(...)` assumed the Kafka runtime had already populated `correlation_id_var`. That was true inside `BaseConsumer.run()`, but false for direct consumer invocation paths, so replay-path idempotency records could fall back to `"<not-set>"` even when message headers already carried a real `correlation_id`. | Extracted reusable header resolution into `BaseConsumer._resolve_message_correlation_id(...)`, updated `TransactionEventConsumer` to set/reset correlation context locally when direct paths do not already have it, and added tests proving replay and stage-gate direct execution persist the Kafka header correlation. | Apply the same pattern to any other consumer whose `process_message(...)` can be invoked directly outside `BaseConsumer.run()`; direct-path audit fidelity should not depend on runtime-loop side effects. | Evidence: [CR-095-REPLAY-CORRELATION-PROPAGATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-095-REPLAY-CORRELATION-PROPAGATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/kafka_consumer.py`; `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/consumers/transaction_event_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_calculator/consumers/test_position_calculator_consumer.py`. |
| CR-096 | 2026-03-11 | Direct consumer correlation context | Hardened | The same direct-path correlation assumption from CR-095 existed more broadly in control-plane and valuation consumers: they read `correlation_id_var` inside `process_message(...)` without ensuring the context had been initialized locally. That could silently degrade audit/idempotency fidelity on direct invocation paths. | Added shared `BaseConsumer._message_correlation_context(...)` to unify current-context reuse, header resolution, deterministic fallback ids, and payload-correlation preference. Applied it to the reviewed high-value control-plane and valuation consumers and extended direct consumer tests accordingly. | Continue applying the same helper to any remaining consumer whose `process_message(...)` is intentionally invoked directly in tests or in-process replay/control paths. | Evidence: [CR-096-DIRECT-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md](./CR-096-DIRECT-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/kafka_consumer.py`; reviewed consumer files under `valuation_orchestrator_service`, `financial_reconciliation_service`, and `pipeline_orchestrator_service`; corresponding unit tests. |
| CR-097 | 2026-03-11 | Watermark advance mismatch visibility | Hardened | Epoch fencing in `bulk_update_states(...)` prevents stale overwrites, but the valuation scheduler still treated partial advancement as routine success. That made stale-epoch skips safe but operationally invisible. | Updated `ValuationScheduler._advance_watermarks(...)` to warn when fewer rows are advanced than were prepared, including prepared/updated/skipped counts and sample keys. Added unit coverage for the partial-advance warning path. | If later metrics work is needed, expose the stale-skipped count as explicit telemetry. The immediate observability gap is now closed at log/test level. | Evidence: [CR-097-WATERMARK-ADVANCE-MISMATCH-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-097-WATERMARK-ADVANCE-MISMATCH-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_valuation_scheduler.py`. |
| CR-098 | 2026-03-11 | Terminal reprocessing normalization | Hardened | The valuation scheduler only considered lagging keys (`watermark_date < latest_business_date`), so a key could remain `status="REPROCESSING"` even after its watermark had already reached the latest business date. That left stale control state and undercounted the reprocessing gauge. | Added `get_terminal_reprocessing_states(...)` to the valuation repository and updated the scheduler to normalize terminal `REPROCESSING` rows back to `CURRENT` via epoch-fenced bulk updates. Added unit coverage for the normalization path. | If later operational reporting needs to distinguish genuinely active reprocessing from just-normalized terminal residue, expose the normalized count as explicit telemetry rather than inferring it indirectly. | Evidence: [CR-098-TERMINAL-REPROCESSING-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-098-TERMINAL-REPROCESSING-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_valuation_scheduler.py`. |
| CR-099 | 2026-03-12 | Terminal reprocessing normalization visibility | Hardened | CR-098 corrected stale terminal `REPROCESSING` rows by normalizing them back to `CURRENT`, but the normalization path still treated partial success as routine. Because `bulk_update_states(...)` is epoch-fenced, terminal normalization can legitimately skip stale rows once a newer epoch already exists. That is safe, but leaving it silent weakens runtime diagnosability in the same way CR-097 previously did for watermark advancement. | Updated `ValuationScheduler._advance_watermarks(...)` to warn when fewer terminal reprocessing states are normalized than were prepared, including prepared count, updated count, stale-skipped count, and sample keys. Added unit coverage for the partial-normalization warning path. | If this path becomes operationally noisy, surface the skipped count as explicit telemetry rather than removing the warning. | Evidence: [CR-099-TERMINAL-REPROCESSING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-099-TERMINAL-REPROCESSING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_valuation_scheduler.py`. |
| CR-100 | 2026-03-12 | Reprocessing fan-out stale skip visibility | Hardened | `ReprocessingWorker` fans out watermark resets across all portfolios holding a security, but its success logging treated partial updates as routine success. Because `PositionStateRepository.update_watermarks_if_older(...)` can legitimately skip rows when newer or already-older state exists, epoch/current-state fencing could silently reduce the actual fan-out without any runtime signal. | Updated `ReprocessingWorker._process_batch()` to warn when fewer watermark resets are applied than were targeted, including targeted count, updated count, stale-skipped count, and sample keys. Added unit coverage for the partial fan-out warning path. | If this path becomes noisy in production, expose the skipped count as explicit telemetry instead of removing the warning. | Evidence: [CR-100-REPROCESSING-FANOUT-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-100-REPROCESSING-FANOUT-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_reprocessing_worker.py`. |
| CR-101 | 2026-03-12 | Back-dated replay duplicate-trigger deduplication | Hardened | When a back-dated original transaction triggered epoch bump and replay, `PositionCalculator.calculate(...)` always appended the triggering event after loading historical transactions from the database. If the triggering transaction had already been persisted to the canonical transaction table, it was replayed twice in the same epoch. | Deduplicated the replay batch by `transaction_id` before appending the triggering event, preserving deterministic ordering while preventing double replay of the same canonical transaction. Added unit coverage for the persisted-trigger replay case. | If future replay flows can legitimately emit the same `transaction_id` with different semantic payloads, deduplication must move to a stronger replay-key contract. For the current canonical transaction model, `transaction_id` is the correct replay identity. | Evidence: [CR-101-BACKDATED-REPLAY-DEDUP-REVIEW.md](./CR-101-BACKDATED-REPLAY-DEDUP-REVIEW.md); `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/core/position_logic.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_calculator/core/test_position_logic.py`. |
| CR-102 | 2026-03-12 | DB-backed replay deduplication proof | Hardened | CR-101 fixed duplicate replay of a persisted triggering transaction at the unit level, but the invariant still needed proof on the real atomic outbox path. Without a DB-backed assertion, the system could regress while still appearing correct under mocks. | Added an integration test that persists the triggering back-dated transaction, runs the real atomic replay path, and asserts the outbox batch contains each `transaction_id` only once. | If future replay batching evolves beyond transaction identity, extend the proof to the stronger replay-key contract rather than relying only on `transaction_id` uniqueness. | Evidence: [CR-102-DB-BACKED-REPLAY-DEDUP-PROOF.md](./CR-102-DB-BACKED-REPLAY-DEDUP-PROOF.md); `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_atomicity.py`. |
| CR-103 | 2026-03-12 | Replay outbox correlation preservation | Hardened | The back-dated replay path created durable outbox events without explicitly propagating the active correlation context into the outbox row. That meant the replay payload could be correct while the durable outbox lineage still lost the triggering correlation id. | Updated `PositionCalculator.calculate(...)` to persist the active `correlation_id_var` value on replay outbox events when present, and added both unit and DB-backed integration coverage proving the outbox rows retain that correlation. | Apply the same durability test pattern to any other outbox-backed replay or fan-out path that currently relies on ambient context rather than explicit outbox correlation propagation. | Evidence: [CR-103-REPLAY-OUTBOX-CORRELATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-103-REPLAY-OUTBOX-CORRELATION-REVIEW.md); `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/core/position_logic.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_calculator/core/test_position_logic.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_atomicity.py`. |
| CR-104 | 2026-03-12 | Dead timeseries scheduler residue | Hardened | After the RFC 81 service split, `timeseries_generator_service` still carried a dead `AggregationScheduler` copy and test even though scheduling ownership had moved to `portfolio_aggregation_service`. The file was no longer imported by production code and only blurred ownership. | Deleted the dead scheduler and its unit test from `timeseries_generator_service`, and corrected current-state feature docs so they attribute aggregation scheduling to `portfolio_aggregation_service`. | Keep scheduler ownership singular. Future aggregation scheduling changes should only land in `portfolio_aggregation_service`. | Evidence: [CR-104-DEAD-TIMESERIES-SCHEDULER-RESIDUE-REVIEW.md](./CR-104-DEAD-TIMESERIES-SCHEDULER-RESIDUE-REVIEW.md); deleted `src/services/timeseries_generator_service/app/core/aggregation_scheduler.py`; deleted `tests/unit/services/timeseries_generator_service/timeseries-generator-service/core/test_aggregation_scheduler.py`; updated `docs/features/timeseries_generator/02_API_Specification_Timeseries_Generator.md`; updated `docs/features/timeseries_generator/04_Operations_Troubleshooting_Guide.md`. |
| CR-105 | 2026-03-12 | Calculator consumer direct-path correlation context | Hardened | Three calculator consumers could be invoked directly without `BaseConsumer.run()`, but still assumed `correlation_id_var` had already been initialized by the runtime loop. That weakened durable outbox/idempotency lineage on direct execution paths. | Applied `_message_correlation_context(...)` to the cashflow, cost, and position-valuation consumers so they resolve Kafka header correlation ids locally on direct paths. Also corrected the `cost_calculator_service` success path so `mark_event_processed(...)` runs after successful publication even when no instrument events exist. Added targeted unit coverage across all three consumers. | Continue applying the same rule to any other direct-invocation consumer path: durable audit fields must not depend on ambient runtime context. | Evidence: [CR-105-CALCULATOR-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md](./CR-105-CALCULATOR-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md); `src/services/calculators/cashflow_calculator_service/app/consumers/transaction_consumer.py`; `src/services/calculators/cost_calculator_service/app/consumer.py`; `src/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/app/consumers/valuation_consumer.py`; calculator consumer unit tests. |
| CR-106 | 2026-03-12 | Portfolio aggregation consumer ownership and correlation context | Hardened | `PortfolioTimeseriesConsumer` still existed in both `portfolio_aggregation_service` and `timeseries_generator_service`, even though only `portfolio_aggregation_service` owned it at runtime. The duplicate implementation and test ownership drift created a real risk of future fixes landing in the dead copy. | Deleted the dead duplicate consumer from `timeseries_generator_service`, moved the unit tests under `portfolio_aggregation_service`, corrected stale file headers, and applied `_message_correlation_context(...)` to the live portfolio aggregation consumer and the adjacent position-timeseries worker so direct invocation preserves Kafka header correlation ids. | Keep aggregation consumer ownership singular. Portfolio aggregation belongs only to `portfolio_aggregation_service`; `timeseries_generator_service` should remain position-timeseries only. | Evidence: [CR-106-PORTFOLIO-AGGREGATION-CONSUMER-OWNERSHIP-REVIEW.md](./CR-106-PORTFOLIO-AGGREGATION-CONSUMER-OWNERSHIP-REVIEW.md); `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/consumers/portfolio_timeseries_consumer.py`; deleted `src/services/timeseries_generator_service/app/consumers/portfolio_timeseries_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/consumers/test_portfolio_timeseries_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/timeseries_generator_service/timeseries-generator-service/consumers/test_position_timeseries_consumer.py`. |
| CR-107 | 2026-03-12 | Persistence consumer direct-path correlation context | Hardened | `GenericPersistenceConsumer` still relied on ambient `correlation_id_var` state for durable outbox/idempotency writes, even though persistence consumers are invoked directly in unit and in-process paths. That weakened durable lineage across the persistence layer. | Updated the shared base consumer to use `_message_correlation_context(...)` and strengthened representative persistence consumer tests so direct invocation preserves the Kafka header correlation id in both outbox publication and `mark_event_processed(...)`. | Keep persistence correlation handling centralized in the base class. Future subtype fixes should not bypass the shared contract. | Evidence: [CR-107-PERSISTENCE-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md](./CR-107-PERSISTENCE-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md); `src/services/persistence_service/app/consumers/base_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/persistence_service/consumers/test_persistence_transaction_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/persistence_service/consumers/test_persistence_instrument_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/persistence_service/consumers/test_persistence_market_price_consumer.py`. |
| CR-108 | 2026-03-12 | Shared direct-consumer correlation helper convergence | Hardened | `PriceEventConsumer` and `TransactionEventConsumer` still implemented direct-path correlation handling manually even after the shared helper existed. That preserved correct behavior but left the contract inconsistent by implementation, increasing drift risk. | Replaced the manual context setup in both consumers with `_message_correlation_context(...)` and verified behavior against the existing direct-path unit suites. | Keep direct consumer correlation handling on the shared helper path. Avoid manual `ContextVar` token management in individual consumers unless there is a demonstrated exception. | Evidence: [CR-108-SHARED-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-108-SHARED-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/consumers/price_event_consumer.py`; `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/consumers/transaction_event_consumer.py`; corresponding unit tests. |
| CR-109 | 2026-03-12 | Cost reprocessing consumer direct-path correlation context | Hardened | `cost_calculator_service` still had one unreviewed direct-invocation consumer: `ReprocessingConsumer`. It republished transactions through `ReprocessingRepository`, but its `process_message(...)` path still relied on ambient `correlation_id_var` state instead of explicitly adopting the Kafka header correlation id. | Wrapped the consumer body in `_message_correlation_context(...)` and added unit coverage proving the repository executes under the header-provided correlation id during direct invocation. | Keep any future replay/reprocessing consumer on the same shared correlation-context contract. Direct paths must not depend on `BaseConsumer.run()` side effects for durable audit lineage. | Evidence: [CR-109-COST-REPROCESSING-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md](./CR-109-COST-REPROCESSING-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md); `src/services/calculators/cost_calculator_service/app/consumers/reprocessing_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/cost_calculator_service/consumer/test_reprocessing_consumer.py`. |
| CR-110 | 2026-03-12 | Set-based `position_state` bulk updates | Hardened | `PositionStateRepository.bulk_update_states(...)` was still issuing one SQL `UPDATE` per row. That preserved correctness, but it was not a gold-standard shape for a hot control table mutated by the valuation scheduler and replay normalization paths. | Replaced the row-by-row loop with a single set-based epoch-fenced update using a derived values table, while preserving the `(portfolio_id, security_id, expected_epoch)` fence and explicit `updated_at = now()` semantics. Added multi-row timestamp integrity coverage. | Keep future bulk control-table mutations set-based unless there is a specific reason not to. Per-row loops on scheduler hot paths should be treated as a performance smell by default. | Evidence: [CR-110-SET-BASED-POSITION-STATE-BULK-UPDATE-REVIEW.md](./CR-110-SET-BASED-POSITION-STATE-BULK-UPDATE-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/position_state_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_position_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-111 | 2026-03-12 | Reprocessing request ordering determinism | Hardened | `ReprocessingRepository.reprocess_transactions_by_ids(...)` queried `transaction_id IN (...)` with no ordering. That made multi-transaction replay requests nondeterministic even though the API accepts an ordered list of transaction ids. | Added explicit ordering by caller-provided `transaction_ids` and added unit coverage proving republished message order matches request order. | Preserve deterministic replay order whenever a replay API accepts multiple ids or events. Row-return order from the database is not a valid replay contract. | Evidence: [CR-111-REPROCESSING-REQUEST-ORDERING-REVIEW.md](./CR-111-REPROCESSING-REQUEST-ORDERING-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_repository.py`. |
| CR-112 | 2026-03-12 | Reprocessing request duplicate-ID deduplication | Hardened | CR-111 fixed deterministic ordering for multi-transaction replay requests, but the replay API still accepted duplicate transaction IDs and would republish the same canonical transaction multiple times in one request. That is a real replay-correctness gap, not just inefficiency. | Deduplicated caller-provided `transaction_ids` while preserving first-seen order before querying and republishing, and added unit coverage proving duplicate IDs result in one republish per canonical transaction in deterministic order. | Keep replay request handling idempotent at the API boundary. If future replay workflows need explicit duplicate semantics, define that as a separate contract rather than falling out of raw input lists. | Evidence: [CR-112-REPROCESSING-REQUEST-DEDUP-REVIEW.md](./CR-112-REPROCESSING-REQUEST-DEDUP-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_repository.py`. |
| CR-113 | 2026-03-12 | Replay API correlation header hygiene | Hardened | The replay API boundary now preserved deterministic ordering and deduplicated duplicate transaction ids, but it still treated the default `correlation_id_var` value `"<not-set>"` as a real correlation header. That could republish replay messages with poisoned lineage metadata even though no real correlation id had been established. | Normalized `"<not-set>"` to `None` before constructing Kafka headers and added unit coverage proving replay publication omits the correlation header when the ambient context is unset. | Apply the same normalization rule to any remaining publication paths that still convert ambient correlation context directly into durable headers. | Evidence: [CR-113-REPLAY-API-CORRELATION-HYGIENE-REVIEW.md](./CR-113-REPLAY-API-CORRELATION-HYGIENE-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_repository.py`. |
| CR-114 | 2026-03-12 | Write-plane lineage sentinel normalization | Hardened | The ingestion write plane still treated the default context sentinel `"<not-set>"` as real lineage metadata. That could leak into Kafka headers and acknowledgement/request-lineage payloads even when no real correlation, request, or trace id had been established. | Normalized `"<not-set>"` to `None` in `IngestionService._get_headers(...)` and in `get_request_lineage()`, then added unit coverage for both the Kafka-header path and the shared request-lineage helper. | Apply the same sentinel-normalization rule to any other remaining API-layer helpers that echo ambient lineage context directly into durable or client-visible metadata. | Evidence: [CR-114-WRITE-PLANE-LINEAGE-SENTINEL-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-114-WRITE-PLANE-LINEAGE-SENTINEL-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); `src/services/ingestion_service/app/services/ingestion_service.py`; `src/services/ingestion_service/app/request_metadata.py`; ingestion-service unit tests. |
| CR-115 | 2026-03-12 | Enterprise readiness lineage sentinel normalization | Hardened | Both enterprise-readiness middleware implementations still treated the default ambient context sentinel `"<not-set>"` as a real correlation id. That meant deny/write audit events could record poisoned client-visible lineage metadata even when no real correlation id had been established. | Added correlation normalization in both enterprise-readiness implementations so `None`, empty string, and `"<not-set>"` collapse to `None` before audit emission, and added unit coverage for deny and successful write audit paths in both owners. | Apply the same normalization rule to any remaining middleware or API helpers that echo ambient lineage state into logs, audits, or client-visible metadata. | Evidence: [CR-115-ENTERPRISE-READINESS-LINEAGE-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-115-ENTERPRISE-READINESS-LINEAGE-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); query-service and query-control-plane enterprise readiness modules; corresponding unit tests. |
| CR-116 | 2026-03-12 | Reprocessing ingress duplicate suppression | Hardened | The replay API consumer and repository were already being hardened for deterministic ordering and duplicate suppression, but the ingress router still accepted duplicate transaction IDs and published duplicate `transactions.reprocessing.requested` commands before the downstream layers saw them. | Deduplicated caller-provided `transaction_ids` at ingress while preserving first-seen order, and used the deduplicated list consistently for rate limiting, job accepted count, persisted request payload, publication, and failure bookkeeping. Added router-level integration coverage proving duplicate IDs produce one command per canonical transaction. | Keep replay request deduplication at the earliest boundary possible. Downstream consumers may still defend themselves, but ingress should not knowingly inject duplicate commands. | Evidence: [CR-116-REPROCESSING-INGRESS-DUPLICATE-SUPPRESSION-REVIEW.md](./CR-116-REPROCESSING-INGRESS-DUPLICATE-SUPPRESSION-REVIEW.md); `src/services/ingestion_service/app/routers/reprocessing.py`; `tests/integration/services/ingestion_service/test_ingestion_routers.py`. |
| CR-117 | 2026-03-12 | Reset-watermarks durable job coalescing | Hardened | Repeated instrument-level reprocessing triggers for the same security could create multiple pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs even before the worker claimed the first one. That inflated the durable queue with redundant work and weakened recovery determinism. | `ReprocessingJobRepository.create_job(...)` now coalesces pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs by `security_id`, updating the pending job to the earliest impacted date instead of creating duplicates. Added unit coverage for both same/later-date coalescing and earlier-date merge behavior. | If duplicate fanout pressure still shows up under production load, consider a database-level uniqueness contract for pending reset-watermarks jobs keyed by `(job_type, payload->>security_id)` instead of relying only on repository behavior. | Evidence: [CR-117-RESET-WATERMARKS-JOB-COALESCING-REVIEW.md](./CR-117-RESET-WATERMARKS-JOB-COALESCING-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-118 | 2026-03-12 | Instrument reprocessing trigger priority order | Hardened | Instrument-level replay triggers were claimed by `updated_at` only. Under backlog, that lets row recency override the actual business priority, which is the oldest impacted date. | `ValuationRepositoryBase.get_instrument_reprocessing_triggers(...)` now orders by `earliest_impacted_date`, then `updated_at`, then `security_id`, and a DB-backed integration test proves the scheduler-facing order is oldest-impact-first. | If replay backlog becomes large enough to need explicit fairness guarantees, consider exposing queue-age and oldest-impacted-date telemetry directly rather than relying only on deterministic ordering. | Evidence: [CR-118-INSTRUMENT-REPROCESSING-PRIORITY-ORDER-REVIEW.md](./CR-118-INSTRUMENT-REPROCESSING-PRIORITY-ORDER-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_instrument_reprocessing_repo.py`. |
| CR-119 | 2026-03-12 | Reset-watermarks worker claim priority order | Hardened | After CR-118, instrument-level triggers were prioritized by oldest impacted date, but the durable `RESET_WATERMARKS` worker queue still claimed work by `created_at` only. That left the replay pipeline with inconsistent priority once work crossed the durable queue boundary. | `ReprocessingJobRepository.find_and_claim_jobs(...)` now claims `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs by `(payload->>'earliest_impacted_date')::date`, then `created_at`, then `id`, while leaving other job types on the generic created-at order. Added repository tests locking both query branches. | If the durable queue later needs stronger starvation protection across job types, move from job-type-specific ordering to an explicit priority model instead of relying on implicit SQL branches. | Evidence: [CR-119-RESET-WATERMARKS-WORKER-CLAIM-PRIORITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-119-RESET-WATERMARKS-WORKER-CLAIM-PRIORITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-120 | 2026-03-12 | Dead instrument reprocessing repository ownership | Hardened | `InstrumentReprocessingStateRepository` still existed in both `valuation_orchestrator_service` and `position_valuation_calculator`, but only the valuation orchestrator copy was live. The worker-local duplicate represented dead alternate ownership for an active replay-trigger contract. | Deleted the dead `position_valuation_calculator` copy and kept ownership singular under `valuation_orchestrator_service`, where back-dated price-event replay triggers actually originate. | Keep instrument-level replay trigger ownership singular. If the repository ever becomes truly shared again, move it to a real shared package instead of restoring service-local duplicates. | Evidence: [CR-120-DEAD-INSTRUMENT-REPROCESSING-REPOSITORY-OWNERSHIP-REVIEW.md](./CR-120-DEAD-INSTRUMENT-REPROCESSING-REPOSITORY-OWNERSHIP-REVIEW.md); deleted `src/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; live owner remains `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-121 | 2026-03-12 | Historical reset-watermarks duplicate normalization | Hardened | CR-117 prevented new duplicate pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs through the repository, but historical duplicates could still already exist in the durable queue and would eventually be processed redundantly by the worker. | Added `normalize_pending_reset_watermarks_duplicates()` and invoked it before `RESET_WATERMARKS` claim. The queue now keeps one pending row per `security_id`, updates the keeper to the minimum impacted date, and deletes redundant pending rows. Added repository tests that lock the normalization and claim sequencing. | If duplicate residue still matters operationally, surface the deleted-count as explicit replay queue telemetry rather than relying only on the log line. | Evidence: [CR-121-HISTORICAL-RESET-WATERMARKS-DUPLICATE-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-121-HISTORICAL-RESET-WATERMARKS-DUPLICATE-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-122 | 2026-03-12 | DB-backed reset-watermarks normalization proof | Hardened | CR-121 hardened the repository logic, but the durable queue behavior still needed proof against the real database path. Without that, normalization remained a unit-level claim rather than a demonstrated SQL contract. | Added DB-backed integration coverage proving duplicate pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` rows collapse to one row per security with the earliest impacted date, while other job types remain untouched by the normalization logic. | Keep durable replay queue semantics proven below the worker layer. If the SQL changes again, this proof should fail before E2E is needed. | Evidence: [CR-122-DB-BACKED-RESET-WATERMARKS-NORMALIZATION-PROOF.md](./CR-122-DB-BACKED-RESET-WATERMARKS-NORMALIZATION-PROOF.md); `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-123 | 2026-03-12 | Instrument trigger coalescing DB proof | Hardened | The live `InstrumentReprocessingStateRepository` is the durable source of instrument-level replay triggers, but its coalescing behavior had not been proven against the real database path. Repeated back-dated price events could have regressed into multiple rows or widened impacted dates without being caught below scheduler/E2E level. | Added DB-backed integration coverage proving repeated upserts leave one trigger row per security and preserve the earliest impacted date, and corrected the stale module header so the file reflects its real owner path. | Keep the trigger-source contract proven below the scheduler layer. If the trigger model evolves, extend these tests rather than relying on scheduler behavior to catch source drift. | Evidence: [CR-123-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-COALESCING-DB-PROOF.md](./CR-123-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-COALESCING-DB-PROOF.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; `tests/integration/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/test_instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-124 | 2026-03-12 | Fenced instrument trigger deletion | Hardened | `ValuationScheduler` converted fetched instrument-level triggers into durable `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs and then deleted trigger rows by `security_id` only. A newer back-dated price event could lower the impacted date between fetch and delete, letting an older scheduler cycle erase the newer replay requirement. | Changed trigger deletion to use `(security_id, earliest_impacted_date)` fences, updated the scheduler to pass the fetched trigger state instead of raw ids, and added DB-backed proof that a stale delete cannot remove a trigger row whose impacted date was lowered after fetch. | Keep trigger consumption fenced by fetched state, not just identity. If trigger rows later gain more mutable state, extend the fence rather than regressing to identity-only deletes. | Evidence: [CR-124-FENCED-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-DELETION-REVIEW.md](./CR-124-FENCED-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-DELETION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_instrument_reprocessing_repo.py`. |
| CR-125 | 2026-03-12 | Pending reset-watermarks uniqueness | Hardened | Duplicate pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` work was already coalesced and normalized in repository logic, but the durable queue still relied on Python behavior rather than schema enforcement. A direct insert path or future regression could still reintroduce duplicate pending replay jobs for the same security. | Added an Alembic migration that normalizes historical duplicate pending rows and creates a partial unique index on pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs by `payload->>'security_id'`. Added DB-backed integration coverage proving the database rejects a second pending row for the same security. | Keep replay-critical uniqueness in the schema where possible. Repository coalescing should remain as the ergonomic path, but the durable queue invariant must now be treated as a DB contract. | Evidence: [CR-125-PENDING-RESET-WATERMARKS-UNIQUENESS-REVIEW.md](./CR-125-PENDING-RESET-WATERMARKS-UNIQUENESS-REVIEW.md); `alembic/versions/e4f5a6b7c8d9_feat_add_pending_reset_watermarks_uniqueness.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-126 | 2026-03-12 | Atomic reset-watermarks creation | Hardened | CR-125 strengthened the queue by enforcing uniqueness in the database, but `ReprocessingJobRepository.create_job(...)` still used a read-then-insert/update pattern. Under concurrency, that could race into an integrity failure instead of coalescing replay intent. | Replaced the read-then-write path with one SQL upsert that inserts or merges a pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` job and returns the surviving row. Added unit and DB-backed integration coverage proving repeated creation leaves one pending row with the earliest impacted date. | Keep schema-backed uniqueness paired with atomic repository behavior. Once the database owns the invariant, repository code should use one atomic write path rather than a best-effort read-modify-write sequence. | Evidence: [CR-126-ATOMIC-RESET-WATERMARKS-CREATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-126-ATOMIC-RESET-WATERMARKS-CREATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-127 | 2026-03-12 | Typed instrument trigger upsert | Hardened | The instrument trigger source was already atomic, but it still embedded the incoming impacted date inside raw SQL text for the `LEAST(...)` merge expression. That was weaker than the queue path we just hardened and left an avoidable raw-SQL seam on an active replay boundary. | Replaced the string-built `LEAST(...)` expression with a typed SQLAlchemy `func.least(...)` upsert using `stmt.excluded.earliest_impacted_date`, while preserving explicit `updated_at = now()` semantics. Re-ran the DB-backed trigger repository proof to verify behavior stayed correct. | Keep replay-trigger upserts typed and explicit. Atomicity alone is not enough; the merge expression should be structurally owned in code rather than assembled from raw SQL fragments. | Evidence: [CR-127-TYPED-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-UPSERT-REVIEW.md](./CR-127-TYPED-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-UPSERT-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; `tests/integration/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/test_instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`. |
| CR-128 | 2026-03-12 | Latest processing-date fallback for scheduler normalization | Hardened | The valuation scheduler assumed `business_dates` always exists. In real workflows, valuation jobs and snapshots can complete without that table being seeded, leaving `get_latest_business_date()` as `None`. That made watermark advancement and terminal `REPROCESSING -> CURRENT` normalization a no-op even though the data plane was already complete. | Changed `ValuationRepository.get_latest_business_date()` to fall back to the latest observed valuation-processing date from `daily_position_snapshots` and `portfolio_valuation_jobs` when the business calendar is empty. Added DB-backed repository proof and reran the exact AVCO -> cashflow E2E sequence that had been leaking `REPROCESSING` state across module cleanup. | Use the business calendar as the primary source of truth, but do not let an empty calendar strand durable replay state when actual valuation work has already completed. Scheduler normalization needs a deterministic latest-processing-date fallback. | Evidence: [CR-128-LATEST-PROCESSING-DATE-FALLBACK-REVIEW.md](./CR-128-LATEST-PROCESSING-DATE-FALLBACK-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_valuation_repo.py`; `tests/e2e/test_5_day_workflow.py`; `tests/e2e/test_avco_workflow.py`; `tests/e2e/test_cashflow_pipeline.py`. |
| CR-129 | 2026-03-12 | Quiescence active-row activity window | Hardened | The quiescence snapshot already excluded non-blocking work correctly, but the quiet-window timestamp still read the max `updated_at` across whole blocking tables. That let historical completed rows hold the quiet window open even when the live blocking snapshot was all zeros, causing false E2E cleanup timeouts. | Changed `read_pipeline_last_activity_at()` to apply the same blocking predicates used by the live snapshot when computing last activity time, and added unit proof that non-blocking and inactive rows do not influence the quiet window. | Keep quiescence logic symmetric: the activity-time window must be derived from the same live blocking set as the snapshot counts, otherwise cleanup becomes sensitive to unrelated historical churn. | Evidence: [CR-129-QUIESCENCE-ACTIVE-ROW-ACTIVITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-129-QUIESCENCE-ACTIVE-ROW-ACTIVITY-REVIEW.md); `tests/test_support/pipeline_quiescence.py`; `tests/unit/test_support/test_pipeline_quiescence.py`. |
| CR-130 | 2026-03-12 | Typed replay-job date binding | Hardened | CR-126 moved pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` creation onto one atomic upsert, but the real asyncpg path still treated the impacted date parameter as ambiguous. The repository contract was correct in shape but not fully valid on the actual driver path. | Explicitly bound `security_id` as `String` and `earliest_impacted_date` as `Date`, and executed the upsert with a parsed `date` value. DB-backed integration proof now passes on the real asyncpg path. | Replay queue hardening is only complete once the atomic path is proven on the real driver, not just in unit tests or abstract SQL form. | Evidence: [CR-130-TYPED-REPLAY-JOB-DATE-BINDING-REVIEW.md](./CR-130-TYPED-REPLAY-JOB-DATE-BINDING-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_job_repository.py`. |
| CR-131 | 2026-03-12 | Integration cleanup contract for replay queue | Hardened | `clean_db` truncated most durable state but omitted `reprocessing_jobs`, so integration tests on the replay queue could inherit historical pending/processing rows and fail for the wrong reason. That made repository tests appear flaky when the issue was test-harness residue. | Added `reprocessing_jobs` to the global truncate list in `tests/conftest.py` and revalidated the replay-job integration suite plus the full integration tier. | Keep `TABLES_TO_TRUNCATE` aligned with every durable control/work queue table as new infrastructure tables are introduced. | Evidence: `tests/conftest.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_reprocessing_job_repository.py`; full `python -m pytest tests/integration -x -q` run green on branch. |
| CR-132 | 2026-03-12 | E2E identity collision and predicate correctness | Hardened | Two broad-suite E2E failures were caused by test-contract issues rather than runtime defects: `test_fx_lifecycle` used a strict-subset check instead of true key containment, and `test_timeseries_pipeline` reused static portfolio/security/transaction identifiers that collided across the full suite. | Fixed the FX cash-position predicate to use `issubset`, randomized the timeseries fixture identifiers with a per-module suffix, and revalidated the exact files plus the full `tests/e2e` tier. | Keep E2E scenarios on unique identities by default and treat broad-suite failures as possible contract-collision defects before assuming pipeline regressions. | Evidence: `tests/e2e/test_fx_lifecycle.py`; `tests/e2e/test_timeseries_pipeline.py`; full `python -m pytest tests/e2e -x -q` run green on branch. |
| CR-133 | 2026-03-12 | Durable replay correlation lineage | Hardened | Back-dated price events preserved correlation on the immediate valuation-job path, but the durable replay chain still lost it: `instrument_reprocessing_state` carried no correlation id and coalesced `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs carried no correlation id. That weakened auditability exactly where durable replay should be strongest. | Added nullable `correlation_id` to both `instrument_reprocessing_state` and `reprocessing_jobs`, propagated the price-event correlation through trigger upsert and scheduler job creation, and proved the earliest impacted trigger owns the persisted correlation on both the trigger row and the durable replay job. | Continue treating durable control rows as first-class lineage records. If future replay intents are coalesced by another key, carry correlation through the durable trigger and the durable job, not just transient event payloads. | Evidence: [CR-125-DURABLE-REPLAY-CORRELATION-LINEAGE-REVIEW.md](./CR-125-DURABLE-REPLAY-CORRELATION-LINEAGE-REVIEW.md); `alembic/versions/f0a1b2c3d4e5_feat_add_replay_correlation_lineage.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; trigger/scheduler unit tests; DB-backed trigger/job integration tests. |
| CR-134 | 2026-03-12 | Coalesced replay lineage backfill | Hardened | Durable replay lineage was preserved when a newer event lowered the impacted date, but it was still dropped when the stored trigger/job row had `correlation_id = NULL` and a later duplicate for the same impacted date arrived with a real correlation id. That left coalesced replay state under-audited even though enough lineage had become available. | Updated both trigger coalescing and pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` job coalescing so `NULL` correlation ids are backfilled from later same-date duplicates, and added DB-backed proofs for both paths. | Treat missing lineage on durable coalesced rows as recoverable state, not as final truth. If better lineage arrives for the same business impact, the durable row should absorb it. | Evidence: [CR-134-COALESCED-REPLAY-LINEAGE-BACKFILL-REVIEW.md](./CR-134-COALESCED-REPLAY-LINEAGE-BACKFILL-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; DB-backed trigger/job integration tests. |
| CR-135 | 2026-03-12 | Reprocessing worker correlation context and dead helper removal | Hardened | Durable replay correlation was preserved on disk, but `ReprocessingWorker` still processed claimed `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs outside the job's correlation context. At the same time, `ValuationRepositoryBase.find_portfolios_for_security(...)` had become dead after the worker switched to date-aware fanout and only survived through obsolete tests. | The worker now executes each durable replay job under `job.correlation_id` and restores ambient context afterward. Added unit proof for that context propagation. Removed the dead `find_portfolios_for_security(...)` helper and its obsolete integration test coverage. | Keep runtime lineage aligned with durable lineage. When a replay helper is no longer part of the real business path, delete it instead of letting old tests preserve fake ownership. | Evidence: [CR-135-REPROCESSING-WORKER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-AND-DEAD-HELPER-REVIEW.md](./CR-135-REPROCESSING-WORKER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-AND-DEAD-HELPER-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; reprocessing worker unit tests; valuation repository integration tests. |
| CR-136 | 2026-03-12 | Date-aware fanout latest-history proof | Hardened | The worker-facing date-aware replay fanout contract had proof for pre-impact closures and future opens, but not for the more realistic mixed case where a portfolio closes before the impacted date and reopens after it. Without that proof, a future query refactor could regress to “any historical holding” semantics. | Added a DB-backed integration test proving `find_portfolios_holding_security_on_date(...)` uses the latest history row on or before the impacted date: the portfolio is excluded while closed on the impacted date and included again after reopening. | Keep the replay fanout contract anchored to the latest holding state as of the impacted date, not any older positive holding record. | Evidence: [CR-136-DATE-AWARE-FANOUT-LATEST-HISTORY-PROOF.md](./CR-136-DATE-AWARE-FANOUT-LATEST-HISTORY-PROOF.md); `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_instrument_reprocessing_repo.py`. |
| CR-137 | Current-Epoch Date-Aware Fanout | Replay / valuation repository | Hardened | `valuation_repository_base.find_portfolios_holding_security_on_date(...)` now fences `PositionHistory` against the live `PositionState.epoch`, and DB-backed proofs now seed explicit current state rows so worker fanout follows the real control model. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_instrument_reprocessing_repo.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-137-CURRENT-EPOCH-DATE-AWARE-FANOUT-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-138 | Valuation Holdings Fixture Current-State Alignment | Test quality / valuation repository | Hardened | Older holdings fixtures were asserting a pre-fence world with no live `PositionState` control rows. The fixtures now seed explicit current state so unit and integration proofs match the real current-epoch repository contract. | `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/repositories/test_unit_valuation_repo.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_valuation_repo.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-138-VALUATION-HOLDINGS-FIXTURE-CURRENT-STATE-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-139 | Position Epoch Bump Fence | Replay / position state machine | Hardened | `increment_epoch_and_reset_watermark(...)` is now fenced by expected epoch, and the stale loser path in `PositionCalculator.calculate(...)` no longer stages replay outbox events. This closes a real concurrent back-dated replay race. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/position_state_repository.py`; `src/services/calculators/position_calculator/app/core/position_logic.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_position_state_repository.py`; `tests/unit/services/calculators/position_calculator/core/test_position_logic.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-139-POSITION-EPOCH-BUMP-FENCE-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-140 | Position History Replay Fanout Index | DB query quality / replay performance | Hardened | Added a replay-fanout-oriented covering index on `position_history` for the worker-facing `(security_id, epoch, latest position_date/id -> portfolio_id)` access path, instead of relying only on the older portfolio-oriented index. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `alembic/versions/f1b2c3d4e5f6_perf_add_position_history_replay_fanout_index.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-140-POSITION-HISTORY-REPLAY-FANOUT-INDEX-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-141 | Atomic Instrument Trigger Claim | Replay / valuation scheduler | Hardened | Replaced the scheduler's fetch-then-delete trigger consumption with a transaction-safe lock-and-delete claim path keyed by the real domain identity (`security_id`). This removes the read/delete race without relying on the unstable one-statement SQL shape. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/integration/services/calculators/position_valuation_calculator/test_int_instrument_reprocessing_repo.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-141-ATOMIC-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-CLAIM-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-142 | Replay Ordering Indexes | DB query quality / replay performance | Hardened | The replay trigger claim and pending `RESET_WATERMARKS` worker claim now use business-correct oldest-impact ordering, but the database still lacked indexes that support those exact orderings. That leaves unnecessary sort/scan pressure on active replay control tables under backlog. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `alembic/versions/f2b3c4d5e6f7_perf_add_replay_ordering_indexes.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-142-REPLAY-ORDERING-INDEX-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-143 | Instrument Trigger Repository Ownership Convergence | Modularity / replay ownership | Hardened | Instrument replay trigger claim/count behavior was still sitting on the shared `ValuationRepositoryBase` even though only the valuation orchestrator owns and uses that path. The apparent “shared” status was being preserved by misplaced tests rather than real production ownership. | `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/valuation_repository.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/test_replay_trigger_repository.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-143-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-REPOSITORY-OWNERSHIP-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-144 | Date-Aware Holdings Repository Ownership Convergence | Modularity / replay ownership | Hardened | The date-aware holdings lookup used by `ReprocessingWorker` still lived on the shared `ValuationRepositoryBase` even though it is only part of the valuation orchestrator replay path. That kept worker-specific fanout logic on the shared surface. | `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/valuation_repository.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py`; `tests/integration/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/test_replay_trigger_repository.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-144-DATE-AWARE-HOLDINGS-REPOSITORY-OWNERSHIP-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-145 | Scheduler Backfill Correlation Scope | Observability / valuation lineage | Hardened | Scheduler-created backfill jobs were stamped with a date-only correlation id, which is too weak for banking-grade lineage because multiple portfolios, securities, and epochs on the same date collapse onto the same pattern. | `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_valuation_scheduler.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-145-SCHEDULER-BACKFILL-CORRELATION-SCOPE-REVIEW.md` |
## Active next batch

1. Continue higher-value orchestrator, scheduler, and replay invariant review, not just observability/document cleanup
2. Review remaining warning-producing or noisy test harness patterns beyond the Kafka/noise and async cleanup fixes in CR-022/CR-023
3. Review remaining replay/watermark terminal-state invariants adjacent to CR-087 through CR-111

| CR-148 | DLQ Correlation Header Normalization Review | Observability / consumer lineage | Hardened | Shared Kafka consumer DLQ emission now normalizes `"<not-set>"` to missing lineage and omits empty Kafka `correlation_id` headers; unit coverage now proves both correlated and uncorrelated DLQ paths. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/kafka_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_kafka_consumer.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-148-DLQ-CORRELATION-HEADER-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md` |
