| CR-150 | Control Queue Hot Path Index Review | Performance / control-table scalability | Hardened | Added composite indexes aligned to replay, valuation, and aggregation claim/stale-reset predicates so hot control-table scans no longer rely on generic single-column coverage. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `alembic/versions/f3c4d5e6f7a8_perf_add_control_queue_hot_path_indexes.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-150-CONTROL-QUEUE-HOT-PATH-INDEX-REVIEW.md` |
| CR-151 | Position State Hot Path Index Review | Performance / replay-valuation control state | Hardened | Added composite indexes aligned to lagging-state, terminal-reprocessing, and watermark-ordered scheduler scans on `position_state`. | `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `alembic/versions/f4d5e6f7a8b9_perf_add_position_state_hot_path_indexes.py`; `docs/architecture/CR-151-POSITION-STATE-HOT-PATH-INDEX-REVIEW.md` |

| CR-152 | 2026-03-12 | Replay duplicate normalization observability | Hardened | Durable replay duplicate normalization had become a meaningful control path, but it was only visible through logs and tests. Operators could not see whether duplicate `RESET_WATERMARKS` rows were actively being collapsed under backlog pressure. | Added `reprocessing_duplicates_normalized_total{scope=...}` to the shared monitoring layer, emitted it from pending reset-watermarks normalization, and surfaced it in Grafana. | Keep future durable replay normalization paths on metric-backed observability, not log-only visibility. | Evidence: [CR-152-REPLAY-DUPLICATE-NORMALIZATION-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-152-REPLAY-DUPLICATE-NORMALIZATION-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`. |
| CR-153 | 2026-03-12 | Replay stale-skip observability | Hardened | The replay and scheduler paths already warned when epoch fencing caused partial updates, but stale-skip counts were not exported as metrics. That made replay pressure invisible unless operators searched logs. | Added `reprocessing_stale_skips_total{stage=...}` to the shared monitoring layer, emitted it from reset-watermarks fanout, watermark advancement, and terminal reprocessing normalization, and surfaced it in Grafana. | Keep stale-skip pressure metric-backed across replay/state-machine paths so operators can see recurring fence pressure before it becomes a support incident. | Evidence: [CR-153-REPLAY-STALE-SKIP-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-153-REPLAY-STALE-SKIP-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`. |
| CR-154 | 2026-03-12 | Shared lineage normalization contract | Hardened | Sentinel lineage normalization was duplicated across transport, write-plane, replay, and audit code. That created drift risk for a banking-critical contract. | Added `normalize_lineage_value(...)` to `portfolio_common.logging_utils` and replaced local sentinel checks across the affected modules, with direct helper proof and affected-surface revalidation. | Keep lineage sentinel handling centralized. New transport or audit boundaries should reuse the shared helper instead of reintroducing local `"<not-set>"` checks. | Evidence: [CR-154-SHARED-LINEAGE-NORMALIZATION-CONTRACT-REVIEW.md](./CR-154-SHARED-LINEAGE-NORMALIZATION-CONTRACT-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/logging_utils.py`; affected write-plane, replay, transport, and audit modules. |
| CR-155 | 2026-03-12 | Outbox terminal failure and hot path | Hardened | `OutboxDispatcher` had `MAX_RETRIES` defined but did not enforce it. Permanently failing rows stayed `PENDING` forever and kept re-entering the live queue. The table also lacked the composite indexes that match the dispatcher's actual claim pattern. | Added terminal failure semantics so retry-threshold rows become `FAILED` instead of remaining `PENDING`, and added `(status, created_at)` plus `(status, last_attempted_at)` indexes for `outbox_events`. Added integration proof for the terminal transition. | If operators later need explicit alerting on terminal outbox failures, add a dedicated Prometheus metric for `FAILED` transitions rather than inferring only from logs. | Evidence: [CR-155-OUTBOX-TERMINAL-FAILURE-AND-HOT-PATH-REVIEW.md](./CR-155-OUTBOX-TERMINAL-FAILURE-AND-HOT-PATH-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/outbox_dispatcher.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `tests/integration/libs/portfolio-common/test_outbox_dispatcher.py`. |
| CR-156 | 2026-03-12 | Outbox backlog and terminal failure observability | Hardened | After CR-155, the dispatcher prevented permanently failing rows from poisoning the live queue, but operators still could not directly see durable `FAILED` residue or the age of the oldest pending row. Existing dashboards only exposed pending count plus published/failed/retried rates. | Added `outbox_events_failed_stored` and `outbox_events_oldest_pending_age_seconds`, updated the dispatcher gauge read to publish them, added DB-backed proof for the gauge contract, and surfaced both metrics on the existing Grafana outbox pressure panel. | If terminal-failure alerting is needed later, derive it from the durable-failure gauge rather than retry-rate heuristics. | Evidence: [CR-156-OUTBOX-BACKLOG-AND-TERMINAL-FAILURE-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-156-OUTBOX-BACKLOG-AND-TERMINAL-FAILURE-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/outbox_dispatcher.py`; `tests/integration/libs/portfolio-common/test_outbox_dispatcher.py`; `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`. |
| CR-157 | 2026-03-13 | Coalesced replay lineage null-earlier-date preservation | Hardened | Durable replay coalescing still had one lineage-loss path: if an older impacted date arrived without a correlation id, it could replace a stored non-null correlation with `NULL` simply because the date was older. That weakened auditability on both the trigger row and the durable replay job. | Updated both trigger and `RESET_WATERMARKS` job coalescing so an earlier incoming row with `correlation_id = NULL` preserves an existing non-null durable correlation. Added DB-backed proofs for both paths. | Keep earliest business impact and best-known lineage as separate concerns. Older date should win ordering, but missing lineage should not erase already-known lineage. | Evidence: [CR-157-COALESCED-REPLAY-LINEAGE-NULL-EARLIER-DATE-REVIEW.md](./CR-157-COALESCED-REPLAY-LINEAGE-NULL-EARLIER-DATE-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; replay lineage DB-backed integration tests. |

| CR-158 | 2026-03-13 | Stale reprocessing retry ceiling and model drift | Hardened | Durable RESET_WATERMARKS jobs could cycle between PROCESSING and PENDING forever after repeated worker crashes, and the SQLAlchemy model still carried the old invalid replay-ordering index expression even though the migration had already been corrected. | Added configurable REPROCESSING_WORKER_MAX_ATTEMPTS with a default of 3, changed stale replay recovery to mark over-limit jobs FAILED with a durable reason instead of resetting forever, passed that ceiling through ReprocessingWorker, and aligned the ReprocessingJob model index expression to the real migration contract. | If operators need explicit alerting on replay jobs that hit the retry ceiling, add a dedicated metric rather than relying only on logs and durable FAILED state. | Evidence: [CR-158-STALE-REPROCESSING-RETRY-CEILING-AND-MODEL-DRIFT-REVIEW.md](./CR-158-STALE-REPROCESSING-RETRY-CEILING-AND-MODEL-DRIFT-REVIEW.md); src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py; src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py; src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/settings.py; src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py; unit replay repository/worker proof. |

| CR-159 | 2026-03-13 | Valuation stale retry ceiling | Hardened | PortfolioValuationJob stale recovery still reset PROCESSING rows back to PENDING forever, leaving the valuation queue inconsistent with the replay queue after CR-158 and allowing repeated worker crashes to create infinite retry cycles. | Added configurable VALUATION_SCHEDULER_MAX_ATTEMPTS with default 3, changed stale valuation recovery to mark over-limit rows FAILED with a durable reason instead of resetting forever, and passed that ceiling through ValuationScheduler. | Apply the same terminal stale-retry symmetry to the aggregation queue so all durable control queues share one failure model. | Evidence: [CR-159-VALUATION-STALE-RETRY-CEILING-REVIEW.md](./CR-159-VALUATION-STALE-RETRY-CEILING-REVIEW.md); src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/valuation_repository_base.py; src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py; src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/settings.py; valuation unit and DB-backed integration proof. |
| CR-160 | 2026-03-13 | Aggregation stale retry ceiling | Hardened | PortfolioAggregationJob stale recovery still reset PROCESSING rows back to PENDING forever and the queue lacked durable retry metadata entirely, leaving aggregation inconsistent with replay and valuation after CR-158 and CR-159. | Added durable `attempt_count` and `failure_reason` to `portfolio_aggregation_jobs`, introduced configurable `AGGREGATION_SCHEDULER_MAX_ATTEMPTS` with default 3, changed stale aggregation recovery to mark over-limit rows FAILED instead of resetting forever, and incremented attempt count on claim. | Surface terminal FAILED aggregation jobs in explicit telemetry if operators need alerting beyond logs and queue inspection. | Evidence: [CR-160-AGGREGATION-STALE-RETRY-CEILING-REVIEW.md](./CR-160-AGGREGATION-STALE-RETRY-CEILING-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/database_models.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/timeseries_repository_base.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/core/aggregation_scheduler.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/settings.py`; `alembic/versions/f5e6f7a8b9c0_feat_add_aggregation_retry_metadata.py`; aggregation unit and DB-backed integration proof. |
| CR-161 | 2026-03-13 | Aggregation job retry metadata visibility | Hardened | CR-160 hardened the durable aggregation queue, but the support/control-plane API still returned aggregation jobs as if they had no retry metadata. That hid active retry and terminal-failure context from operators. | Updated `OperationsService.get_aggregation_jobs(...)` to expose `attempt_count` and `failure_reason`, and strengthened unit and control-plane contract tests to prove those fields are visible on the support API. | Surface aggregation retry/failure pressure in dashboards if row-level support listings are not sufficient for operators. | Evidence: [CR-161-AGGREGATION-JOB-RETRY-METADATA-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-161-AGGREGATION-JOB-RETRY-METADATA-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/query_service/app/services/operations_service.py`; `tests/unit/services/query_service/services/test_operations_service.py`; `tests/integration/services/query_control_plane_service/test_operations_router_dependency.py`. |
| CR-162 | 2026-03-13 | Outbox runtime configurability | Hardened | `OutboxDispatcher` enforced terminal failure semantics, but the retry ceiling still lived as a hardcoded constant inside a shared durable component and the shared polling/batch policy was effectively buried in constructor defaults. That made the banking-critical delivery-control policy opaque and harder to tune safely under load. | Added `OutboxRuntimeSettings` with `OUTBOX_DISPATCHER_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS`, `OUTBOX_DISPATCHER_BATCH_SIZE`, and `OUTBOX_DISPATCHER_MAX_RETRIES`; kept constructor override support for deterministic callers/tests; removed the dead backoff constant; and added both unit and DB-backed proof that terminal failure behavior follows the configured threshold. | If service-specific outbox policy ever diverges, expose it intentionally at the service runtime boundary instead of reintroducing hardcoded forks inside the shared dispatcher. | Evidence: [CR-162-OUTBOX-RETRY-CONFIGURABILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-162-OUTBOX-RETRY-CONFIGURABILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/outbox_dispatcher.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_outbox_dispatcher.py`; `tests/integration/libs/portfolio-common/test_outbox_dispatcher.py`. |
| CR-163 | 2026-03-13 | Stale timeout runtime controls | Hardened | Replay, valuation, and aggregation queues exposed retry ceilings, but the companion stale-processing timeout still lived as a hardcoded repository default (`15 minutes`). That left a key recovery threshold implicit at the data-access layer rather than owned by the runtime that applies it. | Added `VALUATION_SCHEDULER_STALE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES`, `REPROCESSING_WORKER_STALE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES`, and `AGGREGATION_SCHEDULER_STALE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES`, and wired the owning runtimes to pass those values explicitly to stale-reset repository calls. | Keep recovery thresholds runtime-owned. New durable control queues should expose both stale timeout and retry ceiling together instead of hardcoding one side at the repository layer. | Evidence: [CR-163-STALE-TIMEOUT-RUNTIME-CONTROLS-REVIEW.md](./CR-163-STALE-TIMEOUT-RUNTIME-CONTROLS-REVIEW.md); `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/settings.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/valuation_scheduler.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/settings.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/core/aggregation_scheduler.py`. |
| CR-164 | 2026-03-13 | Duplicate-blocked replay observability proof | Hardened | Replay duplicate-blocked outcomes were already surfaced through APIs, alerts, and Grafana, and the shared monitoring layer already defined `ingestion_replay_duplicate_blocked_total`, but there was no lower-level proof that the service path actually incremented the metric on duplicate-blocked audits. | Added direct unit proofs for `IngestionJobService.record_consumer_dlq_replay_audit(...)` covering both `duplicate_blocked` and `failed` status paths, including the non-cross-increment contract between duplicate-blocked and failure counters. | Keep replay-pressure metrics proved at the service boundary so dashboards and alerts remain evidence-backed instead of assumed. | Evidence: [CR-164-DUPLICATE-BLOCKED-REPLAY-OBSERVABILITY-PROOF.md](./CR-164-DUPLICATE-BLOCKED-REPLAY-OBSERVABILITY-PROOF.md); `tests/unit/services/ingestion_service/services/test_ingestion_job_service_guardrails.py`; `src/services/ingestion_service/app/services/ingestion_job_service.py`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`. |
| CR-165 | 2026-03-13 | Epoch fencer service ownership contract | Hardened | `EpochFencer` still allowed `service_name` to default to `"<not-set>"`, which meant a stale-epoch drop on an active replay/control path could silently lose real service ownership in the emitted metric. | Made `service_name` mandatory on `EpochFencer` construction and updated the shared unit proofs to require an explicit owner. | Keep service ownership explicit on control-path observability helpers; do not rely on sentinel defaults for metrics that drive operational triage. | Evidence: [CR-165-EPOCH-FENCER-SERVICE-OWNERSHIP-CONTRACT.md](./CR-165-EPOCH-FENCER-SERVICE-OWNERSHIP-CONTRACT.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing.py`; `tests/unit/libs/portfolio-common/test_reprocessing.py`. |
| CR-166 | 2026-03-13 | Reprocessing worker no-op outcome observability | Hardened | `RESET_WATERMARKS` jobs that found no impacted portfolios were treated as successful completions, but that outcome was only visible in logs. Operators could not distinguish no-op replay completion from real watermark fanout. | Added `reprocessing_worker_jobs_noop_total{job_type, reason}` and emitted it for `no_impacted_portfolios`, with unit proof that no-op jobs do not mutate state and still complete cleanly. | If replay operations need richer operator visibility, surface the no-op counter in Grafana alongside replay claim/completion/failure metrics. | Evidence: [CR-166-REPROCESSING-WORKER-NOOP-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-166-REPROCESSING-WORKER-NOOP-OBSERVABILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`; `tests/unit/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/core/test_reprocessing_worker.py`. |
| CR-167 | 2026-03-13 | Replay no-op dashboard signal | Hardened | `reprocessing_worker_jobs_noop_total{job_type,reason}` was emitted by the valuation reprocessing worker, but operators still could not see it in the live dashboard or the usage guide. The runtime signal existed without an operational consumer. | Added the no-op replay rate to the `Reprocessing Worker Throughput and Backlog` panel and updated the Grafana guide workflow so operators know how to interpret `noop RESET_WATERMARKS / no_impacted_portfolios`. | Keep new runtime observability signals paired with dashboard and runbook coverage; do not stop at metric emission alone. | Evidence: [CR-167-REPLAY-NOOP-DASHBOARD-SIGNAL-REVIEW.md](./CR-167-REPLAY-NOOP-DASHBOARD-SIGNAL-REVIEW.md); `grafana/dashboards/portfolio_analytics.json`; `docs/operations/Grafana-Dashboard-Guide.md`; `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/monitoring.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/core/reprocessing_worker.py`. |
| CR-168 | 2026-03-13 | Durable replay lineage boundary normalization | Hardened | Sentinel lineage normalization had already been enforced across API, consumer, scheduler, and audit boundaries, but the durable replay repositories still accepted caller-provided `correlation_id` values verbatim. A future regression upstream could still persist `"<not-set>"` into replay trigger or job rows. | Normalized `correlation_id` inside `ReprocessingJobRepository.create_job(...)` and `InstrumentReprocessingStateRepository.upsert_state(...)`, with unit and DB-backed proof that sentinel lineage is stored as `NULL`. | Keep durable lineage normalization enforced at the repository boundary, not only at callers. Persistence contracts should remain safe even if an upstream path regresses. | Evidence: [CR-168-DURABLE-REPLAY-LINEAGE-BOUNDARY-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-168-DURABLE-REPLAY-LINEAGE-BOUNDARY-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/reprocessing_job_repository.py`; `src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/app/repositories/instrument_reprocessing_state_repository.py`; unit and integration proofs. |

That left ORM metadata drifting away from the real durable replay invariant. | Added the missing partial unique index declaration to `ReprocessingJob` and added a metadata-level unit test proving the model advertises the same uniqueness contract as the migration-backed database. | Keep durable queue invariants aligned across migration history, ORM metadata, and tests. | CR-172 | 2026-03-13 | Direct consumer correlation context simplification | Hardened | Several direct-invocation consumers had already adopted `_message_correlation_context(...)`, but still re-read `correlation_id_var` inside the context body. That left the code depending on ambient context even after entering the explicit message-scoped contract. | Switched the reviewed consumers to use the correlation id yielded by `_message_correlation_context(...)` directly and removed the now-redundant `correlation_id_var` imports. | Keep direct consumer lineage handling explicit: once a message-scoped context helper exists, consumers should use its yielded value instead of reaching back into ambient context state. | Evidence: [CR-172-DIRECT-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-SIMPLIFICATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-172-DIRECT-CONSUMER-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-SIMPLIFICATION-REVIEW.md); reviewed consumer modules across timeseries, persistence, cashflow, valuation, cost, and position paths. |
| CR-173 | 2026-03-13 | Portfolio aggregation correlation context simplification | Hardened | `PortfolioTimeseriesConsumer` already used `_message_correlation_context(...)`, but still re-read `correlation_id_var` inside the context body. That left one more active aggregation-path consumer coupled to ambient state after entering the explicit message-scoped contract. | Switched the consumer to use the correlation id yielded by `_message_correlation_context(...)` directly and removed the redundant `correlation_id_var` import. | Keep aggregation-path lineage handling aligned with the rest of the reviewed direct consumers: use the yielded message-scoped correlation id, not ambient state lookups. | Evidence: [CR-173-PORTFOLIO-AGGREGATION-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-SIMPLIFICATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-173-PORTFOLIO-AGGREGATION-CORRELATION-CONTEXT-SIMPLIFICATION-REVIEW.md); `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/consumers/portfolio_timeseries_consumer.py`; `tests/unit/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/consumers/test_portfolio_timeseries_consumer.py`. |

New control queues should publish pending, terminal failed, and oldest pending age directly instead of relying only on rate charts. | Evidence: [CR-174-CONTROL-QUEUE-OBSERVABILITY-CONTRACT-REVIEW.md](./CR-174-CONTROL-QUEUE-OBSERVABILITY-CONTRACT-REVIEW.md); monitoring/repository/scheduler/worker files; dashboard and guide updates. |

Strengthened unit and OpenAPI contract tests so the HTTP support surface now proves the same queue-pressure truth as the dashboard. | Consider surfacing replay queue pressure on the same overview once a stable portfolio-scoped replay backlog contract exists. | Evidence: [CR-175-CONTROL-PLANE-QUEUE-PRESSURE-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-175-CONTROL-PLANE-QUEUE-PRESSURE-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); `src/services/query_service/app/dtos/operations_dto.py`; `src/services/query_service/app/services/operations_service.py`; operations unit and control-plane integration tests. |
| CR-176 | 2026-03-13 | HTTP bootstrap lineage header normalization | Hardened | The shared pure-HTTP bootstrap middleware still trusted incoming lineage headers verbatim. If a caller sent `"<not-set>"` or an empty value, services echoed that sentinel back into response headers and log context instead of treating it as missing lineage. | Normalized incoming `X-Correlation-ID`, `X-Request-Id`, and `X-Trace-Id` through `normalize_lineage_value(...)`, generated fresh ids when the incoming values were missing or sentinel, and added integration proofs for `query_service`, `query_control_plane_service`, and `event_replay_service`. | Apply the same boundary rule to any non-HTTP transport that still accepts raw external lineage values before shared runtime or persistence handling. | Evidence: [CR-176-HTTP-BOOTSTRAP-LINEAGE-HEADER-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-176-HTTP-BOOTSTRAP-LINEAGE-HEADER-NORMALIZATION-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/http_app_bootstrap.py`; app-contract integration tests for query, control-plane, and replay services. |

| CR-179 | 2026-03-13 | Analytics export durable lifecycle review | Hardened | Analytics export jobs only persisted terminal failure state for expected `AnalyticsInputError` paths. An unexpected processing exception could roll back the entire job row and erase the durable support/audit record for that export attempt. | Refactored `AnalyticsTimeseriesService.create_export_job(...)` into explicit durable lifecycle steps: reserve job, mark running, and finalize complete or failed in separate transactions. Unexpected exceptions now persist a durable `failed` job with a stable error message before re-raising, and lower-level tests prove both expected and unexpected failure paths. | Keep durable job lifecycles explicit. Long-running or failure-prone control paths should not rely on one large transaction that can erase operational history on exception. | Evidence: `src/services/query_service/app/services/analytics_timeseries_service.py`; `tests/unit/services/query_service/services/test_analytics_timeseries_service.py`; `tests/integration/services/query_control_plane_service/test_analytics_inputs_router_dependency.py`. |
| CR-180 | 2026-03-13 | Analytics export stale-running reuse contract | Hardened | Durable analytics export jobs could remain in `accepted` or `running` if a process died mid-request. The fingerprint reuse path would then keep reusing that stale row forever instead of superseding it with a new export attempt. | Added configurable `LOTUS_CORE_ANALYTICS_EXPORT_STALE_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` and changed analytics export reservation so completed jobs are reused, fresh accepted/running jobs are reused, and stale accepted/running jobs are first marked `failed` with a durable superseded reason before a new job is created. Added unit proofs for both fresh-running reuse and stale-running replacement. | Keep durable reuse contracts time-aware. Fingerprint deduplication is valuable, but it must not pin stale work indefinitely. | Evidence: `src/services/query_service/app/settings.py`; `src/services/query_service/app/services/analytics_timeseries_service.py`; `tests/unit/services/query_service/services/test_analytics_timeseries_service.py`. |

Added repository, service, router, and OpenAPI contract proof. | Keep support-plane queue visibility paired: if aggregate queue health is exposed, the underlying durable job rows should also be queryable through the same operational surface. | Evidence: [CR-181-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-SUPPORT-LISTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-181-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-SUPPORT-LISTING-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/repository/service/router files; operations unit and control-plane integration tests. |

That left the actual durable valuation work item hidden behind a second lookup. | Added `latest_valuation_job_id` and `latest_valuation_job_correlation_id` to both lineage detail and lineage-key listing contracts, extended the lineage repository query to project the latest valuation job identity directly, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI tests. | Keep lineage support surfaces actionable. If a lineage key is blocked by valuation work, expose the concrete durable valuation job directly instead of forcing operators to pivot through a second support listing. | Evidence: [CR-198-LINEAGE-VALUATION-JOB-IDENTITY-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-198-LINEAGE-VALUATION-JOB-IDENTITY-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/repository/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-199 | 2026-03-14 | Reconciliation finding blocking truth | Hardened | Reconciliation findings already exposed severity and structured detail, but the support contract still forced clients and operators to infer whether a finding was publication-blocking. That duplicated control policy on the client side. | Added `is_blocking` and `operational_state` to `ReconciliationFindingRecord`, centralized the blocking rule in `OperationsService`, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep control-plane support objects explicit about blocking truth. If a finding can hold back publication or release, the server contract should say so directly instead of expecting clients to infer it from severity. | Evidence: [CR-199-RECONCILIATION-FINDING-BLOCKING-TRUTH-REVIEW.md](./CR-199-RECONCILIATION-FINDING-BLOCKING-TRUTH-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-200 | 2026-03-14 | Portfolio control stage durable identity | Hardened | Portfolio control stage rows were already first-class support objects, but the contract still omitted the durable row identifier. That left blocking support rows without a stable handle back to the exact persisted control-stage row. | Added `stage_id` to `PortfolioControlStageRecord`, wired `OperationsService.get_portfolio_control_stages(...)` to surface the durable row id directly, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep first-class support objects durably identifiable. If a row is operator-actionable, the support plane should expose the stable durable identifier directly. | Evidence: [CR-200-PORTFOLIO-CONTROL-STAGE-DURABLE-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-200-PORTFOLIO-CONTROL-STAGE-DURABLE-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-201 | 2026-03-14 | Portfolio control stage lifecycle timing | Hardened | The portfolio control stage listing exposed stage status and latest update time, but it still hid `created_at` and `ready_emitted_at`, which are needed to distinguish stages that never progressed from stages that emitted readiness and later regressed. | Added `created_at` and `ready_emitted_at` to `PortfolioControlStageRecord`, wired the control-stage listing to surface both durable timestamps directly, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep support-plane control objects explicit about lifecycle progression. If durable timing exists on the row, expose it when it materially improves operator triage. | Evidence: [CR-201-PORTFOLIO-CONTROL-STAGE-LIFECYCLE-TIMING-REVIEW.md](./CR-201-PORTFOLIO-CONTROL-STAGE-LIFECYCLE-TIMING-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-202 | 2026-03-14 | Support job creation timing visibility | Hardened | The shared support job contract exposed `updated_at`, but it still hid when the durable row was first created. That made it harder to distinguish newly created work from older queue items with limited movement. | Added `created_at` to `SupportJobRecord`, wired valuation, aggregation, and durable replay listings to surface the durable creation timestamp, extended the replay query to select `ReprocessingJob.created_at`, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep shared support job objects explicit about both queue age and recent movement. If multiple durable job families reuse one contract, expose the durable source timestamps once rather than inventing endpoint-specific age workarounds. | Evidence: [CR-202-SUPPORT-JOB-CREATION-TIMING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-202-SUPPORT-JOB-CREATION-TIMING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/repository/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-203 | 2026-03-14 | Analytics export fingerprint visibility | Hardened | Analytics export jobs already persisted `request_fingerprint`, and the runtime used it to decide whether an export should be reused or superseded, but the support-plane listing still hid it. | Added `request_fingerprint` to `AnalyticsExportJobRecord`, wired the analytics export support listing to surface the durable fingerprint directly, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep operator-facing support rows aligned with the runtime’s actual control handles. If a durable fingerprint drives deduplication or supersession, the support plane should expose it directly. | Evidence: [CR-203-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-FINGERPRINT-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-203-ANALYTICS-EXPORT-FINGERPRINT-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-204 | 2026-03-14 | Reconciliation run request ownership visibility | Hardened | Financial reconciliation runs already persisted `requested_by` and `dedupe_key`, but the support-plane listing still hid both. That removed two concrete control handles from operator view: who requested the run and which durable deduplication scope it belonged to. | Added `requested_by` and `dedupe_key` to `ReconciliationRunRecord`, wired the reconciliation support listing to surface both durable fields directly, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep durable request-ownership and deduplication context visible on operator-facing control rows. If the runtime already uses these fields to scope or reuse work, the support plane should expose them directly. | Evidence: [CR-204-RECONCILIATION-RUN-REQUEST-OWNERSHIP-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-204-RECONCILIATION-RUN-REQUEST-OWNERSHIP-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-205 | 2026-03-14 | Replay key creation timing visibility | Hardened | Replay key support rows exposed current watermark, status, and latest update time, but still hid when the durable key row was first created. If durable replay scope is operator-actionable, creation timing should be visible without leaving the support plane. | Evidence: [CR-205-REPLAY-KEY-CREATION-TIMING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-205-REPLAY-KEY-CREATION-TIMING-VISIBILITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-206 | 2026-03-14 | Support job mapping convergence | Hardened | OperationsService rebuilt the shared SupportJobRecord separately for valuation jobs, aggregation jobs, and durable replay jobs. As the shared support contract grew richer, that duplication increased the risk of silent drift between job families. | Extracted _build_support_job_record(...) in OperationsService, switched all three durable job listings to reuse it, and re-ran the support-plane unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract pack. | Keep shared support contracts implemented once. If multiple durable job families reuse the same operator-facing row shape, centralize the mapping before the contract drifts. | Evidence: [CR-206-SUPPORT-JOB-MAPPING-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-206-SUPPORT-JOB-MAPPING-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md); operations service; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-207 | 2026-03-14 | Reprocessing overview and SLO health | Hardened | Replay health in the support overview and calculator SLO surfaces exposed only a raw active-key count. That hid stale replay residue and oldest replay backlog scope even though valuation and aggregation already exposed those summary signals. | Added ReprocessingHealthSummary in OperationsRepository, surfaced stale replay counts and oldest replay watermark date in both SupportOverviewResponse and ReprocessingSloBucket, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep replay health first-class on summary surfaces. | CR-208 | 2026-03-14 | Support overview control stage identity | Hardened | The support overview exposed whether the latest financial reconciliation controls were blocking, but it hid which durable control-stage row was driving that summary and when that row last changed. | Added controls_stage_id and controls_last_updated_at to SupportOverviewResponse, wired both fields from the latest control-stage row in OperationsService.get_support_overview(...), and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep overview summaries anchored to durable owners. If a summary decision depends on one control-stage row, the overview should expose that row identity and freshness without forcing a second support-plane call. | Evidence: [CR-208-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-CONTROL-STAGE-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-208-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-CONTROL-STAGE-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-209 | 2026-03-14 | Support overview stale threshold alignment | Hardened | The support overview silently hardcoded a 15-minute stale threshold while calculator-slos already allowed custom stale thresholds. That created policy drift between two operator-facing support surfaces describing the same runtime state. | Added stale_threshold_minutes query support and response visibility to the support overview route, wired the selected threshold through OperationsService.get_support_overview(...), and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep stale-policy interpretation aligned across support surfaces. If two endpoints classify stale runtime state, they should expose and honor the same threshold contract. | Evidence: [CR-209-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-STALE-THRESHOLD-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md](./CR-209-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-STALE-THRESHOLD-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md); operations router/DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-210 | 2026-03-14 | Calculator SLO failed window alignment | Hardened | calculator-slos exposed ailed_jobs_last_24h, but the runtime used a hardcoded failed-job recency window and the request contract did not let operators choose or verify that policy. | Added ailed_window_hours query and response support to calculator SLO, renamed the bucket field to ailed_jobs_within_window, wired the selected window through OperationsService.get_calculator_slos(...), and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep failed-job recency policy explicit and auditable. If the runtime counts recent failures using a configurable window, the API contract should not hardcode one policy value in the field name. | Evidence: [CR-210-CALCULATOR-SLO-FAILED-WINDOW-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md](./CR-210-CALCULATOR-SLO-FAILED-WINDOW-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md); operations router/DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-211 | 2026-03-14 | Support overview failed window alignment | Hardened | The support overview consumed recent failed-job counts from repository summaries but still hardcoded a 24-hour failed window, hid that policy from operators, and discarded the recent failed-job counts from the response contract. | Added ailed_window_hours query and response support to the support overview route, surfaced recent failed-job counts for valuation/aggregation/analytics export, wired the selected failed window through OperationsService.get_support_overview(...), and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep overview failure pressure explicit and auditable. If the repository already computes recent failed-job counts, the support overview should expose both those counts and the policy window that produced them. | Evidence: [CR-211-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-FAILED-WINDOW-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md](./CR-211-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-FAILED-WINDOW-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md); operations router/DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-212 | 2026-03-14 | Support policy convergence | Hardened | Support-policy defaults and descriptions for stale thresholds and failed windows were duplicated across router query params, service method defaults, and DTO field descriptions. That made support-plane policy drift more likely whenever defaults or wording changed. | Added shared support_policy.py constants for support stale/failure policy, switched router query params, service defaults, and DTO descriptions to reuse them, and re-ran support-plane unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep support-plane policy defined once. If stale and failure windows drive router behavior, service behavior, and response semantics, they should not be duplicated across layers. | Evidence: [CR-212-SUPPORT-POLICY-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-212-SUPPORT-POLICY-CONVERGENCE-REVIEW.md); support policy/DTO/service/router files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

| CR-213 | 2026-03-14 | Support snapshot timestamp consistency | Hardened | The support overview did not expose when the snapshot was generated, and overview/SLO age calculations used multiple implicit datetime.now(...) calls per response. That made support snapshots less auditable and introduced avoidable internal time skew. | Added generated_at_utc to SupportOverviewResponse, captured one reference timestamp per overview response and reused it for backlog-age calculations, reused one captured timestamp in calculator SLO generation, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep operator-facing summary snapshots timestamped and internally consistent. If a response computes age-based signals, those calculations should derive from one captured clock value per response. | Evidence: [CR-213-SUPPORT-SNAPSHOT-TIMESTAMP-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md](./CR-213-SUPPORT-SNAPSHOT-TIMESTAMP-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-214 | 2026-03-14 | Stale-aware support listing policy alignment | Hardened | Stale-aware support listings still silently hardcoded the default stale threshold while support overview and calculator SLO already allowed operators to choose the threshold. The list responses also hid which threshold and snapshot time produced the stale-state fields. | Added `stale_threshold_minutes` query support to stale-aware support listings, added `stale_threshold_minutes` and `generated_at_utc` to stale-aware list responses, wired one captured timestamp through stale/operational-state calculations in the service layer, restored the stable external `status` query contract for valuation and aggregation listings, and strengthened unit, router dependency, and OpenAPI contract tests. | Keep stale-policy semantics aligned across all operator-facing surfaces. If a list exposes `is_stale_*` fields, it should also expose the threshold and snapshot time that produced them. | Evidence: [CR-214-STALE-AWARE-SUPPORT-LISTING-POLICY-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md](./CR-214-STALE-AWARE-SUPPORT-LISTING-POLICY-ALIGNMENT-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service/router files; query-service unit tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-215 | 2026-03-14 | Support summary repository clock consistency | Hardened | The support overview and calculator SLO responses captured one `generated_at_utc` timestamp in the service layer, but the repository health summary queries still computed stale and failed cutoffs from their own internal `datetime.now(...)` calls. That meant one operator snapshot could still be assembled from multiple drifting time windows underneath it. | Added `reference_now` to replay, valuation, aggregation, and analytics-export health summary repository methods; wired the service-layer `generated_at_utc` through to those queries; strengthened service tests to prove the shared clock is forwarded; and added repository tests that prove the generated SQL uses the exact expected stale and failed cutoffs. | Keep operator-facing support snapshots temporally consistent end to end. If the service captures one response timestamp, repository summary queries should not silently compute their own clocks underneath it. | Evidence: [CR-215-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-REPOSITORY-CLOCK-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md](./CR-215-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-REPOSITORY-CLOCK-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md); operations repository/service files; query-service repository and service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-216 | 2026-03-14 | Support listing stale threshold semantics | Hardened | Support listings exposed `stale_threshold_minutes` in their request and response contract, but the shared stale classifier in OperationsService still used a hidden hardcoded 15-minute threshold. That meant callers could request a different threshold, see it echoed back, and still get stale-state classification from the old default. | Removed the hidden class-level 15-minute stale threshold from the stale-classification path, added `stale_threshold_minutes` to the shared support stale helpers and support job record builder, wired caller-selected stale thresholds through valuation, aggregation, analytics export, replay key, and replay job support listing classification, and added targeted unit tests that prove custom thresholds change stale-state and operational-state results. | Keep operator-facing stale policy truthful. If a support listing exposes `stale_threshold_minutes`, that exact value must drive the stale-state and operational-state classification in the response. | Evidence: [CR-216-SUPPORT-LISTING-STALE-THRESHOLD-SEMANTICS-REVIEW.md](./CR-216-SUPPORT-LISTING-STALE-THRESHOLD-SEMANTICS-REVIEW.md); operations service; query-service unit/repository tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-217 | 2026-03-14 | Support listing ordering clock consistency | Hardened | After CR-216, support listing stale-state labels honored caller-selected stale thresholds in the service layer, but the repository list queries still computed stale ordering priority from their own internal `datetime.now(...)` clock. That meant one response could still prioritize rows using one hidden cutoff and label them using another. | Added `reference_now` to valuation, aggregation, analytics export, replay key, and replay job repository listing queries; wired each support listing to pass its captured `generated_at_utc` into the repository ordering path; strengthened service tests to prove the shared clock is forwarded; and added repository SQL tests that prove the ordering cutoff uses the expected reference timestamp. | Keep support listing ordering and labels derived from one clock. If a response prioritizes stale rows, the ordering cutoff must match the timestamp used to classify `is_stale_*` and operational state. | Evidence: [CR-217-SUPPORT-LISTING-ORDERING-CLOCK-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md](./CR-217-SUPPORT-LISTING-ORDERING-CLOCK-CONSISTENCY-REVIEW.md); operations repository/service files; query-service repository and service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-218 | 2026-03-14 | Date-aware replay job support scope | Hardened | The support-plane replay job listing and count scoped jobs to a portfolio by security membership only. That allowed a portfolio to see durable RESET_WATERMARKS jobs for a security even when the portfolio was not actually impacted on the job's earliest_impacted_date. | Added a date-aware portfolio-scope fence for replay job count and listing queries, scoped each job by the latest current-epoch PositionHistory row on or before its impacted date, required the latest holding quantity to be positive, and strengthened repository tests to prove the impacted-date and current-epoch holding fence in generated SQL. | Keep replay support scope aligned with replay execution scope. If the replay worker fans out work only to portfolios holding a security on the impacted date, the support plane should not surface that work to unaffected portfolios. | Evidence: [CR-218-DATE-AWARE-REPLAY-JOB-SUPPORT-SCOPE-REVIEW.md](./CR-218-DATE-AWARE-REPLAY-JOB-SUPPORT-SCOPE-REVIEW.md); operations repository; query-service repository tests; query-service service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-219 | 2026-03-14 | Replay job support type fence | Hardened | The replay job support DTO and operator semantics only model `RESET_WATERMARKS`, but the repository query did not explicitly constrain `job_type`. That meant any future replay job type with a compatible payload shape could leak into an endpoint whose schema and operational meaning are narrower. | Added explicit `ReprocessingJob.job_type == 'RESET_WATERMARKS'` fencing to replay job support count and listing queries and strengthened repository tests to prove the generated SQL carries the durable job-type fence. | Keep support-plane scope explicit. If an endpoint models one durable replay job family, fence it at the query layer instead of relying on informal assumptions about current data. | Evidence: [CR-219-REPLAY-JOB-SUPPORT-TYPE-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-219-REPLAY-JOB-SUPPORT-TYPE-FENCE-REVIEW.md); operations repository; query-service repository tests; query-service service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-220 | 2026-03-14 | Lineage key operational priority ordering | Hardened | The lineage key listing already exposed server-owned health semantics such as `REPLAYING`, `VALUATION_BLOCKED`, `ARTIFACT_GAP`, and `HEALTHY`, but the repository query still ordered keys alphabetically by `security_id`. That let unhealthy keys disappear behind healthy ones on the first page. | Added severity-first ordering to lineage key repository queries, prioritizing replaying keys, then blocked artifact-gap keys, then other artifact-gap keys, while keeping deterministic tie-breaking with latest position-history date and `security_id`; strengthened repository tests to prove the generated SQL carries the severity ordering. | Keep support-plane pagination aligned with support-plane semantics. If a listing exposes server-owned operational states, the first page should prioritize the unhealthy rows operators actually need to triage. | Evidence: [CR-220-LINEAGE-KEY-OPERATIONAL-PRIORITY-ORDERING-REVIEW.md](./CR-220-LINEAGE-KEY-OPERATIONAL-PRIORITY-ORDERING-REVIEW.md); operations repository; query-service repository tests; query-service service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-221 | 2026-03-14 | Support summary oldest work identity | Hardened | The support overview and calculator SLO endpoints exposed backlog counts and oldest dates, but still hid the concrete durable work items behind those backlogs. If an overview or SLO endpoint reports backlog pressure, it should also identify the owning durable work item directly instead of forcing a second discovery call. | Evidence: [CR-221-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-OLDEST-WORK-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md](./CR-221-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-OLDEST-WORK-IDENTITY-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/repository/service files; query-service repository/service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

Operators could see the durable row id and still be forced to page or add secondary filters instead of drilling straight into the owning work item. | Added optional `job_id` filters to valuation, aggregation, analytics export, and replay job listing routes; wired the new filters through `OperationsService`; added repository fencing on durable row id in both count and listing queries; and strengthened repository, service, router dependency, and OpenAPI tests to prove the new drill-through contract. | Keep support identities actionable. If the API exposes durable row ids in summaries and list rows, the same endpoints should accept those ids as direct filters so support flows remain self-contained. | Evidence: [CR-222-SUPPORT-JOB-ID-DRILL-THROUGH-FILTERS-REVIEW.md](./CR-222-SUPPORT-JOB-ID-DRILL-THROUGH-FILTERS-REVIEW.md); operations router/service/repository files; query-service repository/service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

If the latest durable control row is failed, the overview should expose its stored failure reason directly instead of hiding the first diagnostic clue behind another call. | Evidence: [CR-224-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-CONTROL-FAILURE-REASON-REVIEW.md](./CR-224-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-CONTROL-FAILURE-REASON-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

If a summary surfaces the owning durable job id, it should also surface the durable correlation handle needed for log drill-through so operators do not need a second discovery step. | Evidence: [CR-227-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-JOB-CORRELATION-DRILL-THROUGH-REVIEW.md](./CR-227-SUPPORT-SUMMARY-JOB-CORRELATION-DRILL-THROUGH-REVIEW.md); operations repository/DTO/service files; query-service repository/service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |

If the support overview already links the latest reconciliation run, it should also expose the ownership and failure context operators need most before forcing a second support call. | Evidence: [CR-228-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-RECONCILIATION-OWNERSHIP-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md](./CR-228-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-RECONCILIATION-OWNERSHIP-CONTEXT-REVIEW.md); operations DTO/service files; query-service service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-229 | 2026-03-14 | Support overview reconciliation finding pressure | Hardened | The support overview could already identify the latest reconciliation run linked to the latest financial reconciliation control stage, along with its ownership and failure context. But it still could not tell operators whether that linked run had actually produced blocking findings. That forced another pivot into reconciliation findings just to assess whether the linked run had concrete blocking evidence. | Added repository-level `ReconciliationFindingSummary`, implemented `get_reconciliation_finding_summary(run_id)`, surfaced `controls_latest_reconciliation_total_findings` and `controls_latest_reconciliation_blocking_findings` through `SupportOverviewResponse`, and strengthened repository, service, router dependency, and OpenAPI tests to prove the widened overview contract. | Keep control summaries causally useful. If the support overview already links the latest reconciliation run, it should also expose whether that run produced blocking findings before forcing a separate findings call. | Evidence: [CR-229-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-RECONCILIATION-FINDING-PRESSURE-REVIEW.md](./CR-229-SUPPORT-OVERVIEW-RECONCILIATION-FINDING-PRESSURE-REVIEW.md); operations repository/DTO/service files; query-service repository/service tests; query-control-plane integration/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-230 | 2026-04-18 | Early idempotency fence on consumer write paths | Hardened | Several high-value consumers still used a two-step idempotency pattern: read `processed_events`, perform durable work, then insert the processed marker at the end. The uniqueness constraint already existed, but it was being applied too late, leaving a race window where duplicate deliveries could both mutate state before one lost the final insert. | Hardened `IdempotencyRepository` around one schema-backed `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` insert, added explicit `claim_event_processing(...)`, and moved the main persistence, calculator, valuation-orchestrator, reconciliation, and pipeline-stage consumers to claim the fence at the start of the same transaction as durable mutation. Updated unit tests for the new claim-at-start contract and retained atomic end-of-flow marking only on secondary terminal-handling paths that intentionally open a fresh transaction after rollback. | Continue the banking-grade correctness program with stronger cross-table invariants for lots, positions, cashflows, valuations, timeseries, and correction/restatement flows. The early fence closes one material duplicate-delivery race, but full ledger confidence still depends on figure-level reconciliation and append-only correction semantics. | Evidence: [CR-230-EARLY-IDEMPOTENCY-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-230-EARLY-IDEMPOTENCY-FENCE-REVIEW.md); `src/libs/portfolio-common/portfolio_common/idempotency_repository.py`; consumer write paths across persistence/calculator/orchestrator/reconciliation services; unit tests for idempotency, reconciliation requested consumer, valuation readiness consumer, price-event consumer, position-calculator consumer, and cost-calculator consumer. |
| CR-231 | 2026-04-18 | Timeseries integrity reconciliation coverage | Hardened | `ReconciliationService.run_timeseries_integrity(...)` already modeled portfolio-day completeness and arithmetic invariants, but the lower-level tests barely exercised that control path. That meant a regression in portfolio-vs-position aggregate checks could slip through until heavier runtime investigation. | Added direct unit tests for missing portfolio-timeseries rows and for combined snapshot-count completeness plus portfolio-vs-position aggregate mismatch findings, asserting the exact finding types, deltas, and failed summary posture. | Broaden this proof later with DB-backed integration tests tying `daily_position_snapshots`, `position_timeseries`, and `portfolio_timeseries` together on seeded portfolio-day-epoch slices. The current unit coverage closes the most immediate service-level regression gap. | Evidence: [CR-231-TIMESERIES-INTEGRITY-RECONCILIATION-TEST-COVERAGE-REVIEW.md](./CR-231-TIMESERIES-INTEGRITY-RECONCILIATION-TEST-COVERAGE-REVIEW.md); `tests/unit/services/financial_reconciliation_service/test_reconciliation_service.py`; `pytest tests/unit/services/financial_reconciliation_service/test_reconciliation_service.py -q`. |
| CR-232 | 2026-04-18 | Timeseries integrity missing-portfolio DB proof | Hardened | The reconciliation app already proved one DB-backed timeseries-integrity drift case, but it still lacked coverage for the asymmetric failure mode where position-timeseries rows exist and the corresponding `portfolio_timeseries` row is absent. That is exactly the kind of partial aggregation failure the control surface is supposed to catch. | Added a DB-backed integration test that seeds snapshots plus position-timeseries without a portfolio-timeseries row, executes `POST /reconciliation/runs/timeseries-integrity`, and verifies the persisted `missing_portfolio_timeseries` finding and failed summary posture. | Extend DB-backed proof later for the mirror case (`missing_position_timeseries`) and for multi-security portfolio-day rows with non-zero fees/cashflows across more than one position. The current slice closes one important missing-row control gap on the real route/repository path. | Evidence: [CR-232-TIMESERIES-INTEGRITY-DB-PROOF-REVIEW.md](./CR-232-TIMESERIES-INTEGRITY-DB-PROOF-REVIEW.md); `tests/integration/services/financial_reconciliation_service/test_financial_reconciliation_app.py`; `pytest tests/integration/services/financial_reconciliation_service/test_financial_reconciliation_app.py -q`. |
| CR-233 | 2026-04-18 | Timeseries integrity missing-position DB proof | Hardened | After CR-232, the route still lacked the mirror DB-backed proof where a `portfolio_timeseries` row exists without any `position_timeseries` support rows. | CR-340 | 2026-04-18 | Cashflow projection external-movement contract truth | Hardened | `lotus-core#316` stayed open even though the route audit already showed correct future-withdrawal behavior. This route is an operational external-cash outlook, not a generic future settlement forecast for every transaction family. | Evidence: [CR-340-CASHFLOW-PROJECTION-EXTERNAL-MOVEMENT-CONTRACT-TRUTH.md](./CR-340-CASHFLOW-PROJECTION-EXTERNAL-MOVEMENT-CONTRACT-TRUTH.md); cashflow-projection router/DTO updates; repository/service/router/OpenAPI tests. |
| CR-341 | 2026-05-25 | Canonical portfolio aggregation drain parallelism | Hardened | Canonical Workbench live validation exposed that the Core app-local portfolio aggregation path could leave hundreds of `PB_SG_GLOBAL_BAL_001` portfolio-day aggregation jobs draining serially, keeping Core analytics reference stale even after positions, cash, benchmark, and gateway return-path data were otherwise current. | Partitioned only the `portfolio_day.aggregation.job.requested` local topic through a topic-specific override, keyed aggregation messages by `portfolio_id|aggregation_date`, and let the app-local portfolio aggregation service run a bounded consumer pool. Added tests for the topic override, compose contract, consumer pool configuration, and dispatch key. | This improves the real local runtime’s ability to prove the existing canonical workload; it does not relax seed readiness, skip aggregation, or claim broader global universe/source-product depth. | Evidence: `docker-compose.yml`; `tools/kafka_setup.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/consumer_manager.py`; `src/services/portfolio_aggregation_service/app/core/aggregation_scheduler.py`; focused pytest => `16 passed`. |

equested_by and dedupe_key filters to reconciliation-run count/list queries, wired them through OperationsService.get_reconciliation_runs(...) and GET /support/portfolios/{portfolio_id}/reconciliation-runs, updated route descriptions, and strengthened repository SQL tests, service forwarding tests, router dependency tests, and OpenAPI assertions. | Keep reconciliation ownership context actionable. | CR-241 | 2026-03-14 | Stale reset overwrite race across replay, valuation, and aggregation workers | Hardened | Replay, valuation, and aggregation stale-reset paths all used a two-step `select stale rows -> update by id` flow without re-checking current ownership at update time. A worker could complete or re-claim a row between those steps, and the stale sweep could overwrite live durable state back to `PENDING` or `FAILED`. | Added update-time guards requiring `status = 'PROCESSING'` and `updated_at < stale_cutoff` in all three stale-reset repositories, added unit tests proving the guarded update statements, and added DB-backed characterization tests proving a concurrent completion is not overwritten by the stale reset sweep. | Apply the same update-time ownership fence to any future stale-recovery or retry-reset path that first selects rows and then updates them later. Do not rely on the stale scan alone under contention. | Evidence: [CR-241-STALE-RESET-OVERWRITE-RACE-REVIEW.md](./CR-241-STALE-RESET-OVERWRITE-RACE-REVIEW.md); replay/valuation/aggregation repository changes; targeted unit suite => `29 passed`; DB-backed overwrite race proofs for replay, valuation, and aggregation; OpenAPI gate and touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |

| CR-242 | 2026-03-14 | Terminal ownership fence for worker side effects | Hardened | Even after CR-241 fenced stale reset sweeps, late workers could still finish after losing ownership of a `PROCESSING` row. That left a second race: timeout recovery could reset or fail a job, and a late worker could still emit terminal side effects or overwrite terminal status afterward. | Changed replay and valuation terminal status updates to require current `PROCESSING` ownership, changed aggregation terminal updates to do the same, made those update paths return whether ownership was actually claimed, and gated valuation, aggregation, and replay-worker completion side effects behind that claim. Added unit proofs for replay terminal fencing, valuation late-owner suppression, aggregation late-owner suppression, and replay-worker lost-ownership behavior. | Add DB-backed ownership-loss proofs for the most critical worker families where feasible. Keep applying the same ownership-first pattern anywhere a worker can publish durable side effects after a timeout or retry takeover. | Evidence: [CR-242-TERMINAL-OWNERSHIP-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-242-TERMINAL-OWNERSHIP-FENCE-REVIEW.md); worker/consumer/repository updates; targeted unit suite => `35 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |

| CR-243 | 2026-03-14 | Concurrent reconciliation run dedupe proof | Hardened | `ReconciliationRepository.create_run(...)` already used the right optimistic pre-read plus `IntegrityError` fallback pattern for duplicate `dedupe_key` requests, but that behavior had not been proved under real concurrent sessions. The control path was relying on structure rather than contention evidence. | Added a repository unit test proving the `IntegrityError` fallback returns the existing run, and added a DB-backed two-session integration test that forces both callers past the pre-read before insert and proves one durable run is created, one caller receives `created=True`, one receives `created=False`, and both receive the same `run_id`. | Apply the same DB-backed contention-proof pattern to other durable dedupe/upsert paths, especially instrument reprocessing triggers and valuation job upserts. | Evidence: [CR-243-CONCURRENT-RECONCILIATION-DEDUPE-PROOF.md](./CR-243-CONCURRENT-RECONCILIATION-DEDUPE-PROOF.md); reconciliation repository unit test; DB-backed concurrent dedupe integration proof; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |

| CR-244 | 2026-03-14 | Concurrent instrument trigger coalescing proof | Hardened | Instrument reprocessing trigger upsert already had the correct-looking one-row-per-security UPSERT shape with earliest-date coalescing and correlation backfill, but that behavior had only been proved sequentially. The replay chain was still missing contention evidence for duplicate concurrent price-trigger arrivals. | Added a DB-backed two-session integration test that forces concurrent duplicate arrivals for the same security before either session executes the UPSERT, and proved the durable state still converges to one row, the earliest impacted date, and the correlation id associated with that earliest date. | Apply the same contention-proof pattern to valuation job upsert under duplicate scheduler pressure. | Evidence: [CR-244-CONCURRENT-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-COALESCING-PROOF.md](./CR-244-CONCURRENT-INSTRUMENT-TRIGGER-COALESCING-PROOF.md); instrument trigger repository integration test; targeted pytest => `6 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-245 | 2026-03-14 | Concurrent valuation job upsert proof | Hardened | `ValuationJobRepository.upsert_job(...)` already used a correct-looking UPSERT shape for one durable row per logical valuation job, but that behavior had only been proved sequentially.
