| CR-281 | 2026-03-14 | Valuation status vocabulary contract alignment | Hardened | Query-service lineage/support DTO examples and some query-service tests still advertised completion status as `DONE`, while the durable runtime and repository-backed tests use `COMPLETE`. That left the OpenAPI/examples contract teaching a vocabulary drifted away from real runtime state. | Updated query-service DTO examples and query-service unit/router dependency tests to use `COMPLETE` for valuation lineage/support semantics, while keeping operational-state behavior tolerant. This aligned the published contract with the durable status vocabulary the runtime actually emits. | Keep checking for vocabulary drift anywhere DTO examples, router tests, and durable service state can quietly diverge. Contract drift is easy to miss because behavior may still work even while clients are learning the wrong values. | Evidence: [CR-281-VALUATION-STATUS-VOCABULARY-CONTRACT-ALIGNMENT.md](./CR-281-VALUATION-STATUS-VOCABULARY-CONTRACT-ALIGNMENT.md); targeted query-service pytest => `80 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed; `openapi_quality_gate` passed. |
| CR-282 | 2026-03-14 | Support job skipped state truth | Hardened | Valuation jobs can durably end in `SKIPPED_NO_POSITION`, but the query-service support helper still collapsed that real outcome into `COMPLETED`. That made the support plane flatter and less truthful than the runtime state it was supposed to expose. | Updated support-job operational-state derivation to classify `SKIPPED*` statuses as explicit `SKIPPED`, widened the support-job DTO contract, added unit coverage for the helper branch, and added DB-backed integration proof that a real valuation support listing now exposes `SKIPPED_NO_POSITION` as skipped rather than completed. | Keep checking derived support states against durable runtime outcomes so real non-failure terminal states do not get flattened into generic success buckets. This is especially important on operator-facing banking control surfaces where “skipped” and “completed” are materially different. | Evidence: [CR-282-SUPPORT-JOB-SKIPPED-STATE-TRUTH.md](./CR-282-SUPPORT-JOB-SKIPPED-STATE-TRUTH.md); targeted query-service pytest => `56 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed; `openapi_quality_gate` passed. |
| CR-283 | 2026-03-14 | Outbox synchronous publish failure fence | Hardened | `OutboxDispatcher._process_batch_sync()` only treated delivery callbacks as failure inputs. If `KafkaProducer.publish_message(...)` raised synchronously for one row mid-batch, the dispatcher aborted before `flush(...)`, even though earlier rows in the same batch could already be queued in the producer. That left the batch unaccounted and risked ghost or duplicate delivery behavior on later flush cycles. | Fenced synchronous publish exceptions per row inside the batch loop, recorded those rows as failed delivery outcomes instead of aborting the whole batch, preserved `flush(...)` for already-queued rows, and added a DB-backed mixed-batch proof showing queued rows become `PROCESSED` while the synchronously failing row remains `PENDING` with `retry_count = 1`. | Keep reviewing producer-adjacent paths for failure modes that bypass callback-based accounting. At-least-once outbox delivery is only trustworthy if synchronous producer failures are turned into explicit durable outcomes instead of batch-wide aborts. | Evidence: [CR-283-OUTBOX-SYNCHRONOUS-PUBLISH-FAILURE-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-283-OUTBOX-SYNCHRONOUS-PUBLISH-FAILURE-FENCE-REVIEW.md); outbox dispatcher update; outbox delivery-results integration test; targeted pytest and touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-284 | 2026-03-14 | Outbox flush-timeout accounting | Hardened | `OutboxDispatcher._process_batch_sync()` assumed that a non-exceptional `flush(...)` meant every queued row would eventually be represented in `delivery_ack`. In reality `flush(timeout=...)` can return a positive undelivered count without raising, leaving callback-less rows with no retry or failure accounting at all. | Treated `flush(...) > 0` as an explicit failure-accounting path for rows that still lacked delivery callbacks, preserved the normal retry/terminal-failure update flow for those rows, and added a DB-backed proof where one queued row gets a success callback, a second queued row gets none, `flush(...)` returns `1`, and the callback-less row remains `PENDING` with `retry_count = 1`. | Keep checking queue code for “no exception means fully accounted” assumptions. Producer timeout semantics often fail silently unless the batch logic converts callback-less rows into explicit retry outcomes. | Evidence: [CR-284-OUTBOX-FLUSH-TIMEOUT-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-284-OUTBOX-FLUSH-TIMEOUT-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md); outbox dispatcher update; outbox delivery-results integration test; targeted pytest and touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-285 | 2026-03-14 | Ingestion batch partial publish truth | Hardened | `IngestionService` batch publish loops raised `IngestionPublishError` with only the first failing key. In a mid-batch Kafka publish failure, earlier records may already have been published and later records were never attempted, so ingestion job failure history under-described where the batch actually stopped. | Extended `IngestionPublishError` with `published_record_count`, added shared batch failure construction in `IngestionService`, and updated all ingestion batch publishers to persist the full remaining unpublished tail in `failed_record_keys` rather than just the first failing key. Added unit and integration proofs that both the raised error and the stored ingestion-job failure history now reflect the unpublished remainder of the batch. | If we need deeper ingestion observability later, the next step is structured batch-progress metadata such as published count and remaining unpublished count. For now, keep failure evidence honest anywhere a batch can partially succeed before aborting. | Evidence: [CR-285-INGESTION-BATCH-PARTIAL-PUBLISH-TRUTH-REVIEW.md](./CR-285-INGESTION-BATCH-PARTIAL-PUBLISH-TRUTH-REVIEW.md); ingestion service update; ingestion service unit tests; ingestion router integration tests; targeted pytest => `64 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-286 | 2026-03-14 | Portfolio bundle publish progress truth | Hardened | `publish_portfolio_bundle(...)` fans one mixed request into multiple domain publish paths, but on failure the raised `IngestionPublishError` only described the failing sub-publish. That lost causal context about which earlier entity groups had already been emitted and which later groups were never reached. | Added per-entity `published_counts` tracking inside `publish_portfolio_bundle(...)` and wrapped inner `IngestionPublishError` instances with a bundle-level message that includes the already-published entity-group counts at the moment the bundle stopped, while preserving the original `failed_record_keys` and `published_record_count`. Added a unit proof that a bundle failing in the portfolio stage still reports business dates as already published and later groups as not yet emitted. | If we later want richer ingestion-job diagnostics, this bundle progress map is a good precedent for structured progress metadata rather than message-only context. | Evidence: [CR-286-PORTFOLIO-BUNDLE-PUBLISH-PROGRESS-TRUTH-REVIEW.md](./CR-286-PORTFOLIO-BUNDLE-PUBLISH-PROGRESS-TRUTH-REVIEW.md); ingestion service update; ingestion service unit tests; targeted pytest => `65 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-287 | 2026-03-14 | Reprocessing publish accounting | Hardened | `/reprocess/transactions` and the event-replay ingestion retry path both published directly to Kafka with duplicated local loops and `flush(timeout=5)`. Mid-loop synchronous failure marked the whole request with all transaction ids instead of the real unpublished tail, and positive `flush(...)` results were ignored instead of becoming explicit publish failures. | Added shared `IngestionService.publish_reprocessing_requests(...)`, updated direct reprocessing and ingestion-job retry replay to use it, preserved the unpublished tail on synchronous publish failure, and treated positive flush results as explicit publish failure. Added unit proofs for partial publish and flush timeout plus an integration proof that failed reprocessing jobs now persist only the remaining unpublished transaction ids after mid-loop failure. | Keep consolidating direct Kafka publish loops into shared helpers wherever both write paths and replay paths need the same failure semantics. Drift grows quickly when retry and first-write paths account for partial delivery differently. | Evidence: [CR-287-REPROCESSING-PUBLISH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-287-REPROCESSING-PUBLISH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md); ingestion service/reprocessing router/event replay updates; ingestion service unit tests; ingestion router integration tests; targeted pytest => `68 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-288 | 2026-03-14 | Transaction replay republish accounting | Hardened | `ReprocessingRepository.reprocess_transactions_by_ids(...)` republished completed transaction events directly to Kafka in a loop and then called `flush()`. Mid-loop synchronous failure provided no structured truth about the unpublished tail, and positive `flush()` results were ignored instead of becoming explicit replay failures. | Added `ReprocessingReplayError` with `failed_transaction_ids` and `published_record_count`, fenced partial republish failure inside the replay loop, and treated positive `flush()` results as explicit replay failure. Added unit proofs for both failure modes and confirmed the reprocessing consumer still works with the hardened repository semantics. | Apply the same partial-delivery accounting standard to remaining scheduler and dispatcher loops that still rely on bare `publish_message(...)` plus `flush(...)`. Replay and control paths should not have weaker delivery truth than ingestion paths. | Evidence: [CR-288-TRANSACTION-REPLAY-REPUBLISH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-288-TRANSACTION-REPLAY-REPUBLISH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md); reprocessing repository update; repository and consumer unit tests; targeted pytest => `9 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-289 | 2026-03-14 | Scheduler dispatch flush accounting | Hardened | `ValuationScheduler._dispatch_jobs(...)` and `AggregationScheduler._dispatch_jobs(...)` still used direct `publish_message(...)` loops followed by bare `flush(timeout=10)`. Mid-loop synchronous failure aborted before earlier queued jobs were flushed, and positive flush results were ignored instead of becoming explicit dispatch failures. | Updated both scheduler dispatch loops to flush already-queued jobs even after a later publish failure, raise explicit runtime errors that include the remaining undispatched job keys, and treat positive `flush(timeout=10)` results as explicit delivery-confirmation failure. Added unit proofs for both schedulers covering partial-dispatch failure and flush-timeout failure. | Keep applying the same delivery-accounting standard to remaining scheduler and consumer-side Kafka publish loops. “No exception” is not the same thing as “fully delivered and fully accounted.” | Evidence: [CR-289-SCHEDULER-DISPATCH-FLUSH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-289-SCHEDULER-DISPATCH-FLUSH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md); valuation and aggregation scheduler updates; scheduler unit tests; targeted pytest => `20 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-290 | 2026-03-14 | DLQ publish flush accounting | Hardened | `BaseConsumer._send_to_dlq_async(...)` published a DLQ message, called `flush(timeout=5)`, and then immediately recorded the DLQ event. Because `flush(...)` can return a positive undelivered count without raising, the consumer could persist and log DLQ success before Kafka had actually acknowledged the DLQ message. | Treated positive `flush(timeout=5)` results as an explicit failure inside `_send_to_dlq_async(...)`, skipped durable DLQ-event recording on that path, and reused the existing fatal log branch for both flush-timeout and synchronous publish failure. Added unit proofs that neither failure mode records a DLQ event while both still log the fatal send failure. | Keep tightening safety and recovery paths first. If the platform records a DLQ or replay success before Kafka has truly acknowledged delivery, incident evidence becomes less trustworthy exactly when operators most need it. | Evidence: [CR-290-DLQ-PUBLISH-FLUSH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md](./CR-290-DLQ-PUBLISH-FLUSH-ACCOUNTING-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer update; kafka consumer unit tests; targeted pytest => `11 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-291 | 2026-03-14 | DLQ commit gating | Hardened | `BaseConsumer.run()` treated terminal processing failure as “send to DLQ, then commit offset,” while `_send_to_dlq_async(...)` swallowed DLQ publication failure and only logged it. That meant the base consumer could fail processing, fail to publish to DLQ, and still commit the original Kafka offset, creating a real message-loss path. | Changed `_send_to_dlq_async(...)` to return explicit success/failure truth, updated `BaseConsumer.run()` to commit terminal-message offsets only when DLQ send succeeded, and added unit proofs that successful DLQ send still commits while failed DLQ send leaves the offset uncommitted for Kafka redelivery. | Keep checking any recovery or safety wrapper that still treats “logged recovery attempt” as equivalent to “durably succeeded.” Commit decisions should depend on real delivery success, not on best-effort send attempts. | Evidence: [CR-291-DLQ-COMMIT-GATING-REVIEW.md](./CR-291-DLQ-COMMIT-GATING-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer update; kafka consumer unit tests; targeted pytest => `12 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-292 | 2026-03-14 | DLQ replayer commit gating | Hardened | `tools/dlq_replayer.py` replayed the original message from a DLQ record, called `flush(timeout=5)`, logged success, and committed the DLQ offset. Because positive `flush(...)` results were ignored, the tool could fail to get replay delivery confirmation and still commit away the recovery opportunity. | Treated positive `flush(timeout=5)` results as an explicit replay failure in `DLQReplayConsumer.process_message(...)`, so the tool now commits the DLQ message only after replay delivery is actually accounted for. Added integration proof that a timeout leaves the DLQ message uncommitted, because rerunning the tool with the same consumer group replays the same message again successfully. | Keep applying the same delivery-accounting standard to recovery tooling, not just app services. Operator tools are part of the safety story, and they should not be more lossy than the runtime they are meant to recover. | Evidence: [CR-292-DLQ-REPLAYER-COMMIT-GATING-REVIEW.md](./CR-292-DLQ-REPLAYER-COMMIT-GATING-REVIEW.md); DLQ replayer update; tool integration tests; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-293 | 2026-03-14 | Replay publish truth after bookkeeping failure | Hardened | The ingestion replay endpoints used one broad try/except across Kafka publish plus later retry-state and audit writes. If Kafka replay succeeded but bookkeeping failed, the system flattened that outcome into generic failed, and duplicate-block lookup could miss that the replay had already been published. | Split both replay flows into publish and post-publish bookkeeping phases, added a truthful replay audit status `replayed_bookkeeping_failed`, updated duplicate-block lookup to treat that status as already-published success, and tightened unit/integration proofs around the new operator contract. | Keep checking recovery and remediation flows for places where Kafka publish success and later state persistence are still collapsed into one outcome. Operators need to know whether the replay already happened before they retry. | Evidence: [CR-293-REPLAY-PUBLISH-TRUTH-AFTER-BOOKKEEPING-FAILURE.md](./CR-293-REPLAY-PUBLISH-TRUTH-AFTER-BOOKKEEPING-FAILURE.md); ingestion replay router + ingestion job service/DTO updates; targeted pytest => `5 passed` and `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed; `openapi_quality_gate` passed. |
| CR-294 | 2026-03-14 | Ingress bookkeeping truth after publish | Hardened | Batch ingestion routers used one broad try/except across the real publish/persist work and the later `mark_queued(...)` update. If the real work succeeded but queue bookkeeping failed, the routers still marked the job `failed`, flattening a post-publish bookkeeping problem into a false publish/persist failure. | Added `record_failure_observation(...)` to `IngestionJobService`, introduced shared router helper `raise_post_publish_bookkeeping_failure(...)`, and split write-ingress routers into publish/persist and bookkeeping phases so post-success bookkeeping failure now records a non-terminal failure observation and returns explicit `INGESTION_JOB_BOOKKEEPING_FAILED` without falsely terminal-failing the job. | Add one DB-backed proof for the same post-persist bookkeeping failure on the reference-data path so both Kafka-backed and durable-persist ingress variants have runtime evidence. | Evidence: [CR-294-INGRESS-BOOKKEEPING-TRUTH-REVIEW.md](./CR-294-INGRESS-BOOKKEEPING-TRUTH-REVIEW.md); ingestion router + ingestion job service updates; targeted pytest => `6 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed; `openapi_quality_gate` passed. |
| CR-295 | 2026-03-14 | Reference-data bookkeeping failure proof | Hardened | `CR-294` hardened both Kafka-backed and reference-data ingress routers, but only the Kafka-backed transaction path had end-to-end proof. The reference-data side still lacked runtime evidence that a successful persist followed by failed `mark_queued(...)` would preserve durable truth. | Extended the ingestion router integration harness with a fake reference-data ingestion service and added an end-to-end proof for `/ingest/benchmark-definitions` showing that successful persist plus failed queue bookkeeping returns `INGESTION_JOB_BOOKKEEPING_FAILED`, leaves the job non-terminal, records `persist_bookkeeping`, and preserves the persisted payload. | Continue looking for remediation or operator paths where durable work can succeed before local bookkeeping, then add the same truth-preserving split and proof if it is still missing. | Evidence: [CR-295-REFERENCE-DATA-BOOKKEEPING-FAILURE-PROOF.md](./CR-295-REFERENCE-DATA-BOOKKEEPING-FAILURE-PROOF.md); ingestion router integration harness + tests; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-296 | 2026-03-14 | Ingestion shutdown flush truth | Hardened | Ingestion-service shutdown always logged Kafka producer flush success and ignored the return value of `producer.flush(timeout=5)`. A positive undelivered count could therefore still look like a clean shutdown even when Kafka had not confirmed delivery of all buffered messages. | Hardened the ingestion app lifespan shutdown branch so it logs success only when flush returns zero, logs error when undelivered messages remain, and logs exception on raised flush failure. Added an ingestion app contract test proving the timeout branch suppresses the false success log. | Review any remaining service lifecycle hook that still treats best-effort producer flush as unconditional success, especially outside ingestion. | Evidence: [CR-296-INGESTION-SHUTDOWN-FLUSH-TRUTH.md](./CR-296-INGESTION-SHUTDOWN-FLUSH-TRUTH.md); ingestion main app update; app contract pytest => `2 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-297 | 2026-03-14 | Reprocessing tool flush truth | Hardened | `tools/reprocess_transactions.py` logged replay success before evaluating its final `flush(timeout=10)` result. Because the repository can queue earlier publishes before a later failure, that final flush is part of delivery accounting, not just cleanup, and the tool could overstate recovery success. | Added `_flush_or_raise(...)`, moved success logging behind a zero-undelivered final flush, and kept failure-path flush as best-effort cleanup that does not mask the original replay error. Added unit proofs for empty input, success-path flush timeout, and failure-path cleanup flush timeout behavior. | Review remaining service lifecycle hooks for the same standard: never log producer flush success until the undelivered count is actually zero. | Evidence: [CR-297-REPROCESSING-TOOL-FLUSH-TRUTH.md](./CR-297-REPROCESSING-TOOL-FLUSH-TRUTH.md); tool update + unit tests; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-298 | 2026-03-14 | Worker and scheduler shutdown interruptibility | Hardened | `ReprocessingWorker`, `ValuationScheduler`, and `AggregationScheduler` only flipped `_running = False` on shutdown. If stop arrived while they were sleeping between polls, runtime teardown could stall for the full poll interval even though no more useful work remained. | Added `_stop_event` to all three long-poll loops, made `stop()` set both the flag and the event, and replaced bare `asyncio.sleep(...)` with interruptible stop-event waits so shutdown exits immediately after the current poll work completes. Added direct unit proofs that all three loops stop promptly even with long poll intervals. | Review any remaining long-poll runtime component that still treats shutdown as `set flag -> wait for next timeout`, especially outside these three core loops. | Evidence: [CR-298-WORKER-SCHEDULER-SHUTDOWN-INTERRUPT-REVIEW.md](./CR-298-WORKER-SCHEDULER-SHUTDOWN-INTERRUPT-REVIEW.md); worker/scheduler loop updates; targeted pytest => `32 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-299 | 2026-03-14 | Outbox dispatcher shutdown interruptibility | Hardened | `OutboxDispatcher` only flipped `_running = False` on shutdown and then waited for the next poll sleep to expire. If stop arrived just after a batch finished, runtime teardown could stall for the full poll interval even though no more outbox work remained. | Added `_stop_event` to `OutboxDispatcher`, made `stop()` set both the flag and the event, and replaced bare `asyncio.sleep(...)` in `run()` with an interruptible stop-event wait so dispatcher shutdown exits promptly after the current batch completes. Added a direct unit proof that stop interrupts a long poll sleep. | Keep checking shared long-poll runtime helpers for the same delayed-stop pattern, especially infrastructure components reused across services. | Evidence: [CR-299-OUTBOX-DISPATCHER-SHUTDOWN-INTERRUPT-REVIEW.md](./CR-299-OUTBOX-DISPATCHER-SHUTDOWN-INTERRUPT-REVIEW.md); outbox dispatcher update; targeted pytest => `5 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-300 | 2026-03-14 | Consumer commit failure DLQ misclassification | Hardened | `BaseConsumer.run()` handled successful message processing and synchronous offset commit in the same `try` block. If the message was processed correctly but `commit(...)` failed, the code fell into the terminal-processing failure path and attempted to send the message to DLQ, incorrectly treating broker/commit instability as poison-message evidence. | Split processing and commit into separate success phases, moved commit into the `else` branch after successful processing, and changed commit failure handling to log warning and leave the offset uncommitted for Kafka redelivery without sending the message to DLQ. Added a direct unit proof that commit failure no longer triggers DLQ publication. | Review any higher-level wrapper that still conflates broker/commit failures with terminal business-message failures after this base consumer fix. | Evidence: [CR-300-CONSUMER-COMMIT-FAILURE-DLQ-MISCLASSIFICATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-300-CONSUMER-COMMIT-FAILURE-DLQ-MISCLASSIFICATION-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer update; targeted pytest => `13 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-301 | 2026-03-14 | DLQ commit failure runtime fence | Hardened | Even after CR-300, the terminal-error path still committed offsets directly after successful DLQ publication. If that commit raised, the exception escaped the branch and could crash the consumer task even though DLQ publication had already succeeded. | Wrapped the post-DLQ commit in its own fence, logged warning on commit failure, left the offset uncommitted for Kafka redelivery, and prevented task-wide crash or reclassification of the message. Added a direct unit proof for the post-DLQ commit-failure path. | Keep reviewing shared consumer runtime paths so broker/commit instability never turns into task crashes or gets conflated with business-message failure semantics. | Evidence: [CR-301-DLQ-COMMIT-FAILURE-RUNTIME-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-301-DLQ-COMMIT-FAILURE-RUNTIME-FENCE-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer update; targeted pytest => `14 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-302 | 2026-03-14 | Consumer shutdown flush fence | Hardened | `BaseConsumer.shutdown()` called `consumer.close()` and `producer.flush()` without any timeout accounting or exception fence. Shared shutdown could therefore crash on close/flush failure or overstate clean completion when producer flush left undelivered messages behind. | Fenced `consumer.close()` with error logging, changed producer shutdown flush to `flush(timeout=5)`, logged error when undelivered messages remain, logged error when flush raises, and kept shutdown moving in all cases. Added direct unit proofs for undelivered-message and close/flush-failure shutdown behavior. | Keep applying explicit timeout and exception accounting to any remaining shared shutdown hook that still assumes close/flush succeeds silently. | Evidence: [CR-302-CONSUMER-SHUTDOWN-FLUSH-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-302-CONSUMER-SHUTDOWN-FLUSH-FENCE-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer shutdown update; targeted pytest => `16 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-303 | 2026-03-14 | Consumer shutdown wakeup | Hardened | `BaseConsumer.run()` uses blocking `poll(1.0)` calls. Without an explicit wakeup, shutdown can still wait for the poll timeout boundary even after `_running` is already false. | Updated `BaseConsumer.shutdown()` to call `consumer.wakeup()` before `consumer.close()`, fenced wakeup failure with warning logging, and added a direct unit proof that shutdown now actively wakes the underlying consumer before closing it. | A deeper live-loop characterization could be added later if we want integration-level evidence that wakeup reduces real teardown latency, but the shared shutdown contract is now stronger at the base-runtime layer. | Evidence: [CR-303-CONSUMER-SHUTDOWN-WAKEUP-REVIEW.md](./CR-303-CONSUMER-SHUTDOWN-WAKEUP-REVIEW.md); kafka consumer shutdown update; targeted pytest => `17 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-304 | 2026-03-14 | Bounded runtime shutdown | Hardened | `shutdown_runtime_components(...)` claimed to guarantee bounded shutdown but simply awaited `asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)`. A stuck managed task could therefore hang teardown forever across any service using the shared helper. | Added `shutdown_timeout_seconds`, wrapped the final task wait in `asyncio.wait_for(...)`, and on timeout cancel remaining tasks before gathering them again. Added a direct unit proof that a stuck task is force-cancelled after timeout instead of hanging shutdown indefinitely. | If we want richer operator evidence later, log which tasks required forced cancellation after timeout. The important contract correction is now in place: shared shutdown is actually bounded. | Evidence: [CR-304-BOUNDED-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-REVIEW.md](./CR-304-BOUNDED-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-REVIEW.md); runtime supervision update; targeted pytest => `4 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-305 | 2026-03-14 | Runtime shutdown signal failure fence | Hardened | Even after CR-304, one failing consumer `shutdown()` or one failing stop callback could still abort the rest of the shutdown signaling sequence before remaining components were told to stop. Shared teardown was bounded, but not yet resilient to bad shutdown callbacks. | Added optional logger support to `shutdown_runtime_components(...)`, fenced consumer shutdown and stop-callback exceptions, continued signaling the remaining components after earlier failures, and logged the names of tasks that required forced cancellation after timeout. Added direct unit proofs for callback-failure continuation and timeout task-name logging. | Wire service loggers into more call sites over time so these shared teardown logs consistently surface at the service boundary. | Evidence: [CR-305-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-SIGNAL-FAILURE-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-305-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-SIGNAL-FAILURE-FENCE-REVIEW.md); runtime supervision update; targeted pytest => `6 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-306 | 2026-03-14 | Runtime shutdown logger propagation | Hardened | `shutdown_runtime_components(...)` had been hardened to emit callback-failure and forced-cancellation logs, but most `ConsumerManager` call sites still did not pass their service logger through. The shared shutdown evidence therefore existed in code but not at the service boundary where operators would actually look for it. | Updated all service `ConsumerManager` call sites that invoke `shutdown_runtime_components(...)` to pass `logger=logger`, then validated representative runtime-manager packs across persistence, aggregation, valuation orchestrator, and pipeline orchestrator surfaces. | Add a service-level assertion later if we want explicit proof that a shutdown callback failure is emitted through a service logger, not just that logger propagation is wired. | Evidence: [CR-306-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-LOGGER-PROPAGATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-306-RUNTIME-SHUTDOWN-LOGGER-PROPAGATION-REVIEW.md); consumer-manager call-site updates across services; representative runtime pytest => `8 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-307 | 2026-03-14 | Kafka producer close flush truth | Hardened | `KafkaProducer.close()` flushed and discarded the shared producer with no timeout accounting or exception fence. Flush timeout or failure during producer shutdown could therefore be silently flattened into apparent clean close. | Fenced `KafkaProducer.close()` around `flush(timeout)`, logged error when undelivered messages remain, logged error when flush raises, and still cleared the producer reference in all cases. Added direct unit proofs for both undelivered-count and flush-exception shutdown paths. | Look for any remaining shared producer lifecycle path where shutdown happens without useful surrounding service-level breadcrumbs. | Evidence: [CR-307-KAFKA-PRODUCER-CLOSE-FLUSH-TRUTH-REVIEW.md](./CR-307-KAFKA-PRODUCER-CLOSE-FLUSH-TRUTH-REVIEW.md); kafka utils update; targeted pytest => `7 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-308 | 2026-03-14 | Worker runtime startup failure classification | Hardened | `run_instrumented_worker_service(...)` only wrapped `manager.run()` in its shared critical-error path. If Prometheus instrumentation setup failed before the manager started, the service crashed without the standard worker-runtime critical log. | Moved metrics instrumentation into the shared `try/except` so startup instrumentation failure is now classified through the same service-level critical path as runtime crashes, while still re-raising and emitting the shared shutdown log. Added a direct unit proof for instrumentation-startup failure. | If we want richer lifecycle evidence later, add explicit startup-stage labeling in the critical path. The immediate classification gap is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-308-WORKER-RUNTIME-STARTUP-FAILURE-CLASSIFICATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-308-WORKER-RUNTIME-STARTUP-FAILURE-CLASSIFICATION-REVIEW.md); worker runtime update; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-309 | 2026-03-14 | Kafka topic verification library failure classification | Hardened | `ensure_topics_exist(...)` was shared library code but called `sys.exit(1)` on unexpected metadata-verification failures. That bypassed shared runtime classification and made one helper an implicit process terminator. | Introduced `KafkaTopicVerificationError` and replaced `sys.exit(1)` with typed exception raising on unexpected verification failures, while preserving the retry behavior for expected missing-topic conditions. Added direct unit proofs for success, missing-topic retry trigger, and typed unexpected-failure classification. | Add a service-level proof later so one startup path explicitly shows typed topic-verification failure flowing through the shared worker-runtime critical classification. | Evidence: [CR-309-KAFKA-TOPIC-VERIFICATION-LIBRARY-FAILURE-REVIEW.md](./CR-309-KAFKA-TOPIC-VERIFICATION-LIBRARY-FAILURE-REVIEW.md); kafka admin update; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-310 | 2026-03-14 | Typed Kafka startup failure service proof | Hardened | `CR-309` corrected the shared library behavior, but there was still no representative service-level proof that a real `ConsumerManager` startup path would propagate `KafkaTopicVerificationError` cleanly without partially starting runtime components. | Added a valuation-orchestrator consumer-manager runtime proof showing that typed Kafka topic verification failure propagates before tasks start, and that scheduler/reprocessing-worker stop paths are not falsely exercised on this pre-start failure. | No need to duplicate this across every service unless startup paths diverge. One representative proof is sufficient for now. | Evidence: [CR-310-TYPED-KAFKA-STARTUP-FAILURE-SERVICE-PROOF.md](./CR-310-TYPED-KAFKA-STARTUP-FAILURE-SERVICE-PROOF.md); valuation-orchestrator runtime unit test; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-311 | 2026-03-14 | Runtime failure attribution ordering | Hardened | `wait_for_shutdown_or_task_failure(...)` selected an arbitrary completed task when multiple runtime tasks finished together. That could misattribute a real failure to an unrelated cancellation or clean exit if they landed in the same `done` set. | Changed task selection ordering to prefer exception-bearing failures first, then cancelled tasks, then clean exits. Added a direct unit proof that a real failure is still attributed correctly when a cancellation completes at the same time. | No immediate follow-up needed. The important fix is deterministic attribution of the real failing task. | Evidence: [CR-311-RUNTIME-FAILURE-ATTRIBUTION-ORDERING-REVIEW.md](./CR-311-RUNTIME-FAILURE-ATTRIBUTION-ORDERING-REVIEW.md); runtime supervision update; targeted pytest => `7 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-312 | 2026-03-14 | Kafka consumer override validation | Hardened | Shared config parsing allowed some invalid Kafka consumer runtime overrides to slip through until consumer startup, including invalid `auto.offset.reset` values and boolean values for integer settings. | Added explicit allowed values for `auto.offset.reset`, rejected boolean values for integer Kafka settings, normalized valid offset-reset values to lowercase, and added direct unit proofs for the newly fenced config cases. | If we want deeper guardrails later, add explicit range validation for selected integer Kafka settings. The main shared type/enum leak is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-312-KAFKA-CONSUMER-OVERRIDE-VALIDATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-312-KAFKA-CONSUMER-OVERRIDE-VALIDATION-REVIEW.md); config parser update; targeted pytest => `4 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-313 | 2026-03-14 | Kafka consumer override relationship validation | Hardened | Even after CR-312, shared config parsing still accepted impossible or unsafe Kafka consumer override relationships such as non-positive millisecond settings and `heartbeat.interval.ms >= session.timeout.ms`. These errors only surfaced later at consumer startup. | Added positive-integer validation for selected Kafka consumer settings, introduced shared relationship validation for `heartbeat.interval.ms < session.timeout.ms`, and added direct unit proofs for both raw sanitization and env-driven override loading. | If we want to go further, validate additional Kafka override relationships against broker/client constraints. The highest-value timing relationship gap is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-313-KAFKA-CONSUMER-OVERRIDE-RELATIONSHIP-VALIDATION-REVIEW.md](./CR-313-KAFKA-CONSUMER-OVERRIDE-RELATIONSHIP-VALIDATION-REVIEW.md); config parser update; targeted pytest => `7 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-314 | 2026-03-14 | Merged Kafka override relationship fence | Hardened | `CR-313` validated Kafka consumer override relationships inside each source map, but the final merged defaults+group result could still create an invalid relationship across layers. That left a real gap where “validated” inputs could still fail later at runtime after merge. | Added final merged-boundary relationship validation in `get_kafka_consumer_runtime_overrides(group_id)` and added a direct unit proof for the defaults+group heartbeat/session conflict case. | No immediate follow-up needed. The important correction is that validation now runs at the same merged boundary the runtime actually consumes. | Evidence: [CR-314-MERGED-KAFKA-OVERRIDE-RELATIONSHIP-FENCE-REVIEW.md](./CR-314-MERGED-KAFKA-OVERRIDE-RELATIONSHIP-FENCE-REVIEW.md); config parser update; targeted pytest => `8 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-315 | 2026-03-14 | Consumer merged override runtime proof | Hardened | `CR-314` fenced invalid merged Kafka consumer override relationships in the shared config helper, but there was still no proof that `BaseConsumer` construction preserved that fence when applying runtime overrides into `_consumer_config`. | Added a direct `BaseConsumer` construction proof showing that merged `session.timeout.ms` is applied while an invalid merged `heartbeat.interval.ms` is dropped, preserving the safe built-in heartbeat default. | No immediate follow-up needed. The key point is that the merged override fence is now proven at the actual shared runtime boundary. | Evidence: [CR-315-CONSUMER-MERGED-OVERRIDE-RUNTIME-PROOF.md](./CR-315-CONSUMER-MERGED-OVERRIDE-RUNTIME-PROOF.md); kafka consumer unit proof; targeted pytest => `18 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-316 | 2026-03-14 | Service-level merged override fence proof | Hardened | `CR-315` proved merged Kafka override fencing at `BaseConsumer` construction, but there was still no representative service-level proof that a real `ConsumerManager` startup path preserved that boundary when constructing multiple consumers with different group ids. | Added a valuation-orchestrator consumer-manager proof showing that merged `session.timeout.ms` defaults apply while an invalid merged `heartbeat.interval.ms` override is dropped and the safe built-in heartbeat default is retained on the real consumer instances. | No immediate follow-up needed. One representative service-level proof is enough unless other startup paths diverge materially. | Evidence: [CR-316-SERVICE-MERGED-OVERRIDE-FENCE-PROOF.md](./CR-316-SERVICE-MERGED-OVERRIDE-FENCE-PROOF.md); valuation-orchestrator runtime unit proof; targeted pytest => `4 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-317 | 2026-03-14 | Outbox runtime setting fallback truth | Hardened | Shared `outbox_settings.py` silently clamped invalid or non-positive env values to `1`, which hid bad operator config and could change dispatcher behavior in surprising ways instead of falling back to the documented defaults. | Changed outbox runtime parsing to fall back to the validated default rather than clamping to `1`, and added warning evidence for invalid and non-positive env values. Added direct unit proof that invalid env settings preserve defaults and emit fallback evidence. | If we want to go further later, apply the same operator-truth standard to other shared env-backed runtime settings. The immediate hidden-clamp gap is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-317-OUTBOX-RUNTIME-SETTING-FALLBACK-TRUTH.md](./CR-317-OUTBOX-RUNTIME-SETTING-FALLBACK-TRUTH.md); outbox settings update; targeted pytest => `6 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-318 | 2026-03-14 | Shared config import-time integer fence | Hardened | Shared config still used raw `int(os.getenv(...))` for `BUSINESS_DATE_MAX_FUTURE_DAYS` and `CASHFLOW_RULE_CACHE_TTL_SECONDS` at module import time. Invalid env values could therefore crash service startup across the platform before runtime supervision or service-level failure classification even began. | Added shared `_env_int(...)` parsing with fallback and warning evidence, and routed both import-time settings through it so invalid or out-of-range env values fall back safely instead of poisoning shared startup. Added direct helper proofs and a real module-reload proof that invalid env no longer breaks import. | Apply the same standard later to any remaining import-time typed env parsing in shared config if more settings become runtime-tunable. The immediate startup-fragility gap is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-318-SHARED-CONFIG-IMPORT-TIME-INTEGER-FENCE.md](./CR-318-SHARED-CONFIG-IMPORT-TIME-INTEGER-FENCE.md); config update; targeted pytest => `11 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-319 | 2026-03-14 | Shared config boolean fallback truth | Hardened | Shared config still parsed `BUSINESS_DATE_ENFORCE_MONOTONIC_ADVANCE` with ad hoc string membership logic at import time, so invalid operator values like `maybe` were silently treated as `false` with no evidence. | Added shared `_env_bool(...)` parsing with explicit true/false vocab and warning evidence for invalid values, and routed the monotonic business-date guardrail through it. Added direct helper proofs and a module-reload proof that invalid boolean env no longer silently changes startup policy without evidence. | Apply the same standard later to any other shared boolean env parsing that becomes policy-significant. The immediate hidden-bool-coercion gap is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-319-SHARED-CONFIG-BOOLEAN-FALLBACK-TRUTH.md](./CR-319-SHARED-CONFIG-BOOLEAN-FALLBACK-TRUTH.md); config update; targeted pytest => `13 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-320 | 2026-03-14 | Lazy DB engine initialization fence | Hardened | Shared `portfolio_common.db` created sync and async SQLAlchemy engines at module import time, so invalid DB URLs, missing drivers, or bootstrap-time environment issues could crash imports before any service-level runtime supervision or startup classification began. | Moved sync and async engine/sessionmaker creation behind lazy cached helpers while preserving the existing `SessionLocal()` and `AsyncSessionLocal()` usage pattern. Added direct unit proofs that module import no longer creates engines and that sync/async engines are created only when first used. | Follow up later with a service-level proof if we want one representative startup path that now survives shared DB import before first DB use. The immediate import-time startup fragility is now closed. | Evidence: [CR-320-LAZY-DB-ENGINE-INITIALIZATION-FENCE.md](./CR-320-LAZY-DB-ENGINE-INITIALIZATION-FENCE.md); db bootstrap update; targeted pytest => `3 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-321 | 2026-03-14 | Service import proof for lazy DB fence | Hardened | `CR-320` removed import-time engine creation from shared `portfolio_common.db`, but there was still no representative service-level proof that a real service module importing `SessionLocal` from that shared module no longer triggered engine creation at import time. | Added a direct `query_service.capabilities_service` import proof showing that reloading the real service module after reloading `portfolio_common.db` does not create sync or async SQLAlchemy engines until first DB use. | One representative proof is enough unless another service imports shared DB bootstrap differently. The important point is that the lazy DB fence now holds at a real service boundary, not just inside the shared module. | Evidence: [CR-321-SERVICE-IMPORT-PROOF-FOR-LAZY-DB-FENCE.md](./CR-321-SERVICE-IMPORT-PROOF-FOR-LAZY-DB-FENCE.md); query-service unit proof; targeted pytest => `10 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-322 | 2026-03-14 | Health dependency index alignment fix | Hardened | Shared `create_health_router(...).readiness_probe()` gathered readiness checks only for known dependencies but mapped statuses back by indexing into the original dependency tuple. If an unknown dependency appeared between valid ones, the status mapping could misalign or raise `IndexError`. | Resolved known dependencies into one ordered `(name, check)` sequence and used that same sequence for both `asyncio.gather(...)` and dependency status mapping. Added direct unit proof for mixed known/unknown dependencies and the all-unknown edge case. | No immediate follow-up needed. The main correctness risk was the mismatch between gathered result order and status-mapping order. | Evidence: [CR-322-HEALTH-DEPENDENCY-INDEX-ALIGNMENT-FIX.md](./CR-322-HEALTH-DEPENDENCY-INDEX-ALIGNMENT-FIX.md); health helper update; targeted pytest => `2 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-323 | 2026-03-14 | Database URL scheme compatibility fence | Hardened | Shared DB helpers only normalized `postgresql://` and `postgresql+asyncpg://` URLs. A common `postgres://...` database URL would therefore pass through broken and fail sync or async engine initialization across services. | Added shared DB URL scheme normalization that upgrades `postgres://` to `postgresql://`, converts sync URLs to `postgresql+asyncpg://` for async mode, and strips `+asyncpg` back to `postgresql://` for sync mode. Added direct proofs for both sync and async `postgres://` normalization. | No immediate follow-up needed. The highest-value compatibility hole was the common `postgres://` scheme variant. | Evidence: [CR-323-DATABASE-URL-SCHEME-COMPATIBILITY-FENCE.md](./CR-323-DATABASE-URL-SCHEME-COMPATIBILITY-FENCE.md); db helper update; targeted pytest => `5 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-324 | 2026-03-14 | Shared lineage whitespace normalization fence | Hardened | Shared `normalize_lineage_value(...)` only treated exact `""` and `"<not-set>"` as unset. Whitespace-only values or padded sentinel values could therefore leak through as fake correlation, request, or trace ids across HTTP middleware, logging, and repository lineage persistence. | Changed shared lineage normalization to strip whitespace, treat empty-after-strip and case-insensitive `<not-set>` as unset, and return trimmed real lineage values. Added direct unit proofs for whitespace-only and padded sentinel cases. | No immediate follow-up needed. The main correctness issue was fake lineage leaking through because normalization was too literal. | Evidence: [CR-324-SHARED-LINEAGE-WHITESPACE-NORMALIZATION-FENCE.md](./CR-324-SHARED-LINEAGE-WHITESPACE-NORMALIZATION-FENCE.md); logging utils update; targeted pytest => `4 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-325 | 2026-03-14 | HTTP trace-id validation fence | Hardened | Shared HTTP middleware accepted any non-empty `X-Trace-Id` header and emitted it into `traceparent`. Malformed incoming trace ids could therefore propagate invalid tracing context across services. | Added shared `normalize_trace_id(...)` validation that only accepts 32-character hex trace ids, lowercases valid values, and forces generation of a new trace id when the incoming value is malformed. Added direct unit proof plus one real query-service integration proof. | Extend the same integration proof to another service only if middleware behavior diverges. The important shared boundary is now fenced. | Evidence: [CR-325-HTTP-TRACE-ID-VALIDATION-FENCE.md](./CR-325-HTTP-TRACE-ID-VALIDATION-FENCE.md); http bootstrap update; targeted pytest => `23 passed`; touched-surface `ruff check` passed. |
| CR-326 | 2026-03-15 | Container build baseline hardening | Hardened | `lotus-core` already had multi-stage service Dockerfiles, but the build baseline still carried an underspecified `.dockerignore` and no explicit BuildKit/buildx enablement in Docker-backed CI jobs. That left unnecessary Docker context transfer and weaker image-build acceleration on the heaviest pipeline paths. | Expanded `.dockerignore` to exclude repo metadata, caches, coverage/task-run output, docs, tests, and other non-build inputs. Enabled `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1` and `COMPOSE_DOCKER_CLI_BUILD=1` in CI and added `docker/setup-buildx-action@v3` to Docker-backed jobs. Published the reusable container-build standard and templates into `lotus-platform` so the same baseline can be rolled out across Lotus repos. | Standardize service Dockerfiles onto one shared pattern, then add locked dependency artifacts and cache import/export for Docker builds. | Evidence: [CR-326-CONTAINER-BUILD-BASELINE-HARDENING.md](./CR-326-CONTAINER-BUILD-BASELINE-HARDENING.md); `.dockerignore`; `.github/workflows/ci.yml`; `lotus-platform/platform-standards/Container-Build-and-Image-Engineering-Standard.md`; `lotus-platform/platform-standards/templates/.dockerignore.backend.template`; `lotus-platform/platform-standards/templates/Dockerfile.python-service.template`. |
| CR-327 | 2026-03-15 | Service Dockerfile BuildKit cache mounts | Hardened | Service Dockerfiles already used multi-stage images, but repeated `pip install` steps still ran without BuildKit cache mounts. Heavy Docker-backed gates therefore kept paying unnecessary package download and wheel-build cost across the service fleet. Build validation also made an existing deeper issue explicit: several service packages downgrade or override `portfolio-common` dependency pins inside the same image. | Added `# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7`, `PIP_DISABLE_PIP_VERSION_CHECK=1`, and `RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip ...` to the service Dockerfiles so repeated Docker builds can reuse the pip cache through BuildKit. Validated the pattern with real query-service and valuation-orchestrator image builds. | Next serious build-hardening target: align `portfolio-common` and service dependency versions so image builds stop succeeding with explicit resolver-conflict warnings. | Evidence: [CR-327-SERVICE-DOCKERFILE-BUILDKIT-CACHE-MOUNTS.md](./CR-327-SERVICE-DOCKERFILE-BUILDKIT-CACHE-MOUNTS.md); service Dockerfiles; `docker build -f src/services/query_service/Dockerfile -t lotus-core-query-service-buildcheck .`; `docker build -f src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/Dockerfile -t lotus-core-valuation-orchestrator-buildcheck .`. |
| CR-328 | 2026-03-15 | Shared library dependency convergence | Hardened | Real image builds showed that multiple services were installing older versions of `pydantic`, `sqlalchemy`, `asyncpg`, and `confluent-kafka` after `portfolio-common` had already installed newer pinned versions. That left the images internally inconsistent even though the builds still completed. | Aligned all service `pyproject.toml` files with `portfolio-common` for the overlapping shared runtime dependencies and revalidated representative query and worker image builds. | Move from convergence to locked dependency artifacts and, if possible, centralize shared pins to reduce future drift. | Evidence: [CR-328-SHARED-LIBRARY-DEPENDENCY-CONVERGENCE.md](./CR-328-SHARED-LIBRARY-DEPENDENCY-CONVERGENCE.md); service `pyproject.toml` updates; `docker build -f src/services/query_service/Dockerfile -t lotus-core-query-service-buildcheck .`; `docker build -f src/services/valuation_orchestrator_service/Dockerfile -t lotus-core-valuation-orchestrator-buildcheck .`. |
| CR-329 | 2026-03-15 | Shared build constraints fence | Hardened | After dependency convergence, `lotus-core` still lacked one enforced install boundary for the shared runtime stack. Local bootstrap and Docker builds depended on package metadata only, with no canonical constraints artifact to fence overlapping runtime dependencies. | Added `constraints/shared-build-constraints.txt`, wired `scripts/bootstrap_dev.py` and service Dockerfiles to use it, and aligned `tests/requirements.txt` for the shared async/http dependencies. Validated the constrained install path with representative query and worker image builds. | Move from shared constraints to fuller lock artifacts where feasible; longer term, reduce cross-service framework divergence so broader locking becomes practical. | Evidence: [CR-329-SHARED-BUILD-CONSTRAINTS-FENCE.md](./CR-329-SHARED-BUILD-CONSTRAINTS-FENCE.md); `constraints/shared-build-constraints.txt`; `scripts/bootstrap_dev.py`; service Dockerfiles; `tests/requirements.txt`; representative Docker builds passed. |
| CR-330 | 2026-03-15 | CI tooling lock fence | Hardened | Even after shared runtime constraints were added, `make install` still installed lint/typecheck/security tooling by floating package names. | CR-331 | 2026-03-15 | Wheel-based runtime image fence | Hardened | The service Dockerfiles were multi-stage, but they still copied source trees and builder-installed environments into final images. Final service images no longer copy service source trees or builder workspaces directly. | Longer term, combine this wheel-based runtime fence with fuller lock artifacts once service runtime drift is reduced enough to make broader locking practical. | Evidence: [CR-331-WHEEL-BASED-RUNTIME-IMAGE-FENCE.md](./CR-331-WHEEL-BASED-RUNTIME-IMAGE-FENCE.md); service Dockerfiles; representative Docker builds for query-service and valuation-orchestrator passed. |

| CR-707 | 2026-06-01 | Request-session async DB read boundary | Hardened | PR Merge Gate Docker smoke and latency gates exposed that recent `asyncio.gather(...)` query-service slices started concurrent repository reads on the same request-scoped SQLAlchemy `AsyncSession`, causing runtime `InvalidRequestError` / `IllegalStateChangeError` failures on holdings, transactions, and support overview paths. | Reverted same-session DB fan-out to deterministic sequential awaits across affected holdings, transactions, operations support, cash balances, reporting, liquidity ladder, cashflow projection/evidence, market coverage, and analytics-horizon orchestration; converted focused tests from concurrency-start assertions to ordering assertions that encode the request-session boundary. | Restore production correctness for bank-facing API read paths while preserving query-volume, predicate, streaming, and redundant-read performance hardening. Future DB-level read parallelism must use an explicit separate-session executor with lifecycle, pool, timeout, isolation, and observability controls. No API route shape, database schema, wiki source, or platform contract changed. | Evidence: [CR-707-REQUEST-SESSION-ASYNC-DB-READ-BOUNDARY.md](./CR-707-REQUEST-SESSION-ASYNC-DB-READ-BOUNDARY.md); local validation passed: focused query-service service proof across affected surfaces. |

| CR-1317 | 2026-07-04 | Application command/result contracts | Hardened | GitHub issue #642 showed application services using API DTOs as use-case input/output contracts, coupling HTTP response shapes to workflow behavior and making worker/batch usage depend on API DTO packages. | Added upload application commands/results, lookup application query/results, and a core snapshot identity command; migrated `UploadIngestionService` and `LookupCatalogService` to application contracts; added router mapping helpers that preserve public upload and lookup DTO responses; replaced core snapshot request fingerprinting from API DTO serialization with canonical command payloads; and wired `application-command-result-guard` into `make architecture-guard`. | Existing upload and lookup route paths, request/response DTOs, OpenAPI metadata, upload validation/publish behavior, lookup filtering/sorting behavior, core snapshot fingerprint payload shape, database schema, Kafka contracts, and runtime topology are unchanged. Broader API DTO usage in application services remains transitional backlog. | Evidence: [CR-1317-APPLICATION-COMMAND-RESULT-CONTRACTS.md](./CR-1317-APPLICATION-COMMAND-RESULT-CONTRACTS.md); local validation passed: focused upload/lookup/core-snapshot service and router mapping tests; direct command/result guard; scoped Ruff lint/format; upload integration subset; and lookup integration contract tests. |
| CR-1318 | 2026-07-04 | Application layer contract | Hardened | GitHub issue #641 showed that `lotus-core` had application slices and ports, but no first-class application-layer contract defining what belongs in use cases, command handlers, workflow policies, ports, and error mapping. | Added `docs/standards/application-layer-contract.md`; linked it from `docs/architecture.md`; added `scripts/application_layer_contract_guard.py`; wired `application-layer-contract-guard` into `make architecture-guard`; and added guard tests proving framework/infrastructure imports are blocked from `app/application` and `app/use_cases` packages. | No route path, request/response DTO, OpenAPI metadata, application behavior, repository SQL, database schema, Kafka contract, metric name, or runtime topology changed. Legacy `app/services` modules remain incremental migration scope, while new use cases should follow the application package contract. | Evidence: [CR-1318-APPLICATION-LAYER-CONTRACT.md](./CR-1318-APPLICATION-LAYER-CONTRACT.md); local validation passed: focused application-layer guard tests; direct guard; scoped Ruff lint/format; architecture guard; wiki validation; and diff check. |
| CR-1370 | 2026-07-05 | Kafka consumer execution profiles | Hardened | GitHub issue #575 showed shared Kafka consumers had hidden poll cadence and no first-class worker execution profile for in-flight limits, partition ordering, shutdown drain budget, or overload behavior. | Added `KafkaConsumerExecutionProfile`, default/group override loading, profile-owned polling, bounded cross-partition concurrency with one active message per partition, ordered-pending poll pause behavior, and in-flight/idle/backlog metrics. | Existing consumers remain serial by default; no API contract, database schema, Kafka topic/event payload, persistence model, or runtime topology changed. Future consumer throughput tuning should use the shared profile instead of service-local poll/concurrency settings. | Evidence: [CR-1370-KAFKA-CONSUMER-EXECUTION-PROFILES.md](./CR-1370-KAFKA-CONSUMER-EXECUTION-PROFILES.md); local validation passed: focused shared Kafka consumer and config tests. |
| CR-1371 | 2026-07-05 | Cashflow rule cache governance | Hardened | GitHub issue #576 showed the cashflow rule cache had TTL and process-local invalidation but no rule-set version/effective timestamp, source-version stale-read check, distributed invalidation policy, or cache metrics. | Added `CashflowRuleSetVersion`, repository source-version query, version/effective metadata on cache state and cached rules, source-version verification before fresh-cache hits, explicit cache event metrics, and documented stale-read/invalidation policy. | No API contract, database schema, Kafka topic/event payload, cashflow output, persistence model, or runtime topology changed. Rule changes now invalidate cached rules before TTL expiry when `cashflow_rules.updated_at` changes. | Evidence: [CR-1371-CASHFLOW-RULE-CACHE-GOVERNANCE.md](./CR-1371-CASHFLOW-RULE-CACHE-GOVERNANCE.md); local validation passed: focused cashflow consumer and repository tests. |
